diet: Herbivore (larvae) — horseshoe vetch; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: The Adonis blue butterfly is one of the most vivid blue butterflies in Europe. The intense blue colour is structural, produced by microscopic scales that refract light, not by blue pigment.
threats: Habitat loss (chalk grassland), loss of grazing, climate change
description: The Adonis blue is a small butterfly. Males have brilliant iridescent blue upperwings with white-fringed edges; females are brown with orange spots. Both sexes have distinctive white-fringed, spotted underwings.

habitat: Lowland rainforests of Central and West Africa
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Entandrophragma; Adults — nectar, minerals
interesting_fact: The African giant swallowtail is the largest butterfly in Africa and one of the largest in the world, with a wingspan of up to 25 cm. It is toxic — its body contains poison from its larval food plants, deterring predators.
description: The African giant swallowtail has a wingspan of up to 25 cm. Its wings are orange-brown with black markings and small, pale spots. It is toxic to predators and rarely collected due to its large size and rarity.

diet: Carnivore — antelopes, baboons, hares, birds
interesting_fact: Leopards are the strongest climbers among big cats and habitually haul carcasses heavier than themselves up into trees to cache them from lions and hyenas.
threats: Habitat loss, prey depletion, retaliatory killing, illegal trade
description: The African leopard is the nominate subspecies of the leopard. It has a pale yellow to golden coat covered with dark rosette spots. It is solitary and nocturnal, with extraordinary adaptability across habitats.

habitat: Woodland edges, hackberry groves, stream valleys
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — hackberry; Adults — nectar, sap, mud
interesting_fact: Snout butterflies get their name from their elongated labial palps (mouthparts) that extend forward, making the butterfly look as if it has a long snout. These resemble a broken twig and aid in camouflage when at rest.
description: The American snout butterfly has an orange and dark brown upper wing pattern with white forewing spots. When at rest with wings folded, its extended palps make it remarkably resemble a dead leaf on a twig.

habitat: Tropical rainforests of Central and South America
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various plants; Adults — rotting fruit, dung, sap
interesting_fact: The '88 butterfly' gets its name from the pattern on its hindwing underside, which resembles the number '88' or '89'. This precise, repeatable pattern has fascinated naturalists and collectors for centuries.
description: The 88 butterfly has striking blue-black and red upperside wings. The underside of the hindwings displays the famous black-on-white '88' numeral pattern. It is most visible at rest with wings folded.

diet: Herbivore — grasses, leaves, shrubs
interesting_fact: Antelopes can reach speeds of up to 100 km/h and are capable of jumping 3 metres high to escape predators.
threats: Habitat loss, hunting, competition with livestock
description: Antelopes are a diverse group of herbivorous mammals in the family Bovidae. Most species are native to Africa. They are characterised by their hollow, unbranched horns and slender, graceful builds.

habitat: Alpine meadows, rocky slopes, subalpine zones
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — stonecrop (Sedum); Adults — nectar from alpine flowers
geographic_range: Mountains of Europe and Central Asia
interesting_fact: The apollo butterfly is the only butterfly specifically listed on CITES Appendix II, restricting its international trade. After mating, the male glues a hardened plug (sphragis) to the female's abdomen to prevent her from mating again.
threats: Climate change (shrinking alpine habitat), overgrazing, collection
description: The apollo is a large, striking butterfly with white wings that are semi-transparent with grey vein patterns, black spots near the wing margins, and distinctive red spots with white centres on the hindwings.

habitat: Prairies, meadows, grasslands with aster plants
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — aster (Symphyotrichum); Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: Flower moths of the genus Schinia are cryptically coloured to match the specific flower species their larvae feed on. The arcigera flower moth is found almost exclusively on aster flowers, virtually disappearing against the petals.
description: The arcigera flower moth has distinctive pinkish-purple hindwings and mottled, cryptic forewings with pink and grey-brown colouring. It rests on aster flowerheads during the day, relying on camouflage for protection.

habitat: Open country, grasslands, farmlands, light forests
diet: Carnivore — bees, wasps, dragonflies, other flying insects
geographic_range: Sub-Saharan Africa, Middle East, South and Southeast Asia
interesting_fact: Before eating a bee, the green bee-eater beats it against a branch to remove the sting and squeeze out the venom sac, making the bee safe to eat — a learned behaviour observed across the species.
threats: Habitat loss, pesticide use reducing prey
description: The Asian green bee-eater is a small, brilliantly coloured bird with emerald-green plumage, a blue chin, black eye mask, chestnut crown and nape, and elongated central tail feathers. It is an aerial insect hunter.

habitat: Coastal scrub, pine flatwoods with coontie palm
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — coontie (Zamia integrifolia); Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: The atala butterfly was thought to be extinct in Florida by the 1960s due to destruction of its host plant, coontie. Conservation of coontie in gardens and parks led to a dramatic recovery, making it one of butterfly conservation's success stories.
description: The atala is a small but spectacular butterfly with iridescent blue-green wings bordered in black above. The underside is black with rows of blue spots and a brilliant red abdomen — warning colours advertising its toxicity from coontie.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various trees; Adults — cannot eat (no functional mouthparts)
interesting_fact: The Atlas moth is among the largest moths in the world with a wingspan of up to 30 cm. Adult moths have no functional mouthparts and cannot eat — they survive entirely on fat reserves accumulated as caterpillars and typically live for just 1–2 weeks.
description: The Atlas moth has enormous, richly patterned wings in rusty red and brown with transparent 'windows' and distinctive snake-head patterns at each forewing tip — thought to deter predators by mimicking the head of a snake.

habitat: Tropical forests, forest edges, open areas
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — passionflower vines; Adults — nectar, pollen
interesting_fact: Heliconian butterflies can incorporate amino acids from pollen into their diet — an unusual ability for butterflies. This supplementary protein significantly extends their lifespan (up to six months) compared to most butterflies that live only a few weeks.
threats: Deforestation, loss of passionflower host plants
description: The banded orange heliconian is a long-winged butterfly with brilliant orange-red wings crossed by bold black bands and bordered in black. Its vivid colouration serves as aposematic warning of its toxicity.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — citrus plants; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: The banded peacock butterfly is known to engage in hilltopping — males fly to the tops of hills and tree canopies to establish territories and compete for females who arrive to mate at these elevated vantage points.
description: The banded peacock is a large, striking swallowtail with iridescent blue-green wings crossed by a broad black band and decorated with brilliant iridescent scaling that shimmers in changing light.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various low-growing plants; Adults — minimal
interesting_fact: Tiger moths produce ultrasonic clicks that jam the echolocation signals of hunting bats — a remarkable anti-predator adaptation. The moths with the most vivid warning colours also tend to produce the most effective jamming signals.
description: The banded tiger moth has cream-coloured wings crossed by bold black stripes on the forewings and brilliant red-orange hindwings that flash when the moth is disturbed — a startling warning to predators of its toxicity.

diet: Omnivore — fish, berries, honey, insects, mammals
interesting_fact: Bears are powerful swimmers and some species, such as the polar bear, can swim for hundreds of kilometres without rest.
description: Bears are large mammals in the family Ursidae. They have stocky bodies, rounded ears, short tails and strong claws. Most species are omnivorous and several hibernate during winter.

diet: Herbivore — bark, leaves, aquatic plants
interesting_fact: Beavers are nature's engineers — their dams create wetland habitats that support hundreds of other species and reduce flooding downstream.
description: The North American beaver is the largest rodent in North America. Known for building dams from branches and mud, beavers are semiaquatic with webbed hind feet and a large, flat, paddle-shaped tail.

diet: Unknown
interesting_fact: The Beckers White is one of 209 species in the AniX wildlife identification dataset.
description: The Beckers White is included in the AniX training dataset. Detailed biological information will be updated in a future version.

diet: Herbivore — nectar, pollen
geographic_range: Worldwide (domesticated); native to Europe, Africa, Western Asia
interesting_fact: Honey bees communicate the location of food sources through a complex 'waggle dance' — a figure-8 movement whose direction and duration encode the direction and distance of flowers from the hive. This is one of the most sophisticated forms of communication in the animal kingdom.
threats: Pesticides (especially neonicotinoids), habitat loss, pathogens, climate change
description: The western honey bee is the world's most important commercial pollinator. Workers have pollen baskets on their hind legs, barbed stings, and communicate via the famous waggle dance. They live in highly organised social colonies of up to 80,000 individuals.

habitat: Hedgerows, gardens, woodland edges with bird cherry
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — bird cherry leaves; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: Colonies of bird cherry ermine moth caterpillars can completely envelop entire trees — sometimes entire hedgerows — in dense silken webbing as they feed, creating ghostly white shrouded landscapes that are spectacular but temporary.
threats: Minimal — pest of ornamental bird cherry trees
description: The bird cherry ermine moth has white forewings dotted with rows of small black spots and pale hindwings. Its caterpillars are social web-spinners, covering their host trees in dense communal silk tents while feeding.

diet: Herbivore — grasses, sedges, shrubs
interesting_fact: American bison once numbered 30–60 million across North America. Hunting reduced them to fewer than 1,000 by the 1890s. Conservation efforts have brought numbers back to around 500,000 today.
description: The American bison is the largest land animal in North America. It has a massive head, high shoulder hump, and shaggy dark-brown fur on the front body that becomes lighter toward the rear.

habitat: Woodland rides and edges with blackthorn shrubs
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — blackthorn (Prunus spinosa); Adults — privet and bramble nectar
interesting_fact: The black hairstreak is one of the rarest butterflies in England, confined to the ancient blackthorn hedges and woodland edges of a small area between Oxford and Peterborough. Adults spend much of their short lives in the tree canopy.
threats: Habitat loss, blackthorn removal, lack of woodland management
description: The black hairstreak is a small, dark butterfly with orange-spotted hindwings and a fine hairstreak line on the underside. It is inconspicuous and sedentary, spending most of its time in blackthorn canopy.

diet: Omnivore — earthworms, berries, insects, seeds
geographic_range: Europe, Asia, North Africa; introduced in Australia, New Zealand
interesting_fact: Male blackbirds are renowned for their complex, melodic song — one of the most varied and beautiful of any European bird. They often sing at dawn and dusk, and can incorporate sounds from their environment into their songs.
threats: Cat predation, habitat loss, pesticide reduction of invertebrates
description: The common blackbird is a member of the thrush family. The male has all-black plumage with a bright yellow-orange beak and eye-ring; the female is brown with streaked underparts. It is one of the most familiar garden birds in Europe.

habitat: Tropical rainforests of Central and South America
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — legumes; Adults — rotting fruit, tree sap, fungi
interesting_fact: The blue morpho's wings contain no blue pigment — the brilliant iridescent blue is created by microscopic light-refracting scales. This structural colour is so intense that it is visible from aircraft flying over the rainforest canopy.
description: The blue morpho is one of the world's most spectacular butterflies, with a wingspan of 12–20 cm and wings of brilliant iridescent blue above. The undersides are cryptic brown with eyespots — when flying, the alternating blue/brown creates a flickering camouflage.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various plants; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: Crow butterflies sequester toxic alkaloids from their larval host plants, making themselves unpalatable to predators. Males gather minerals from moist soil and decaying matter to produce pheromones for mating.
description: The blue-spotted crow is a dark brown to black butterfly with rows of white and blue spots on its wings. It flies slowly with shallow wingbeats, advertising its unpalatability with conspicuous colouring.

diet: Unknown
interesting_fact: The Brookes Birdwing is one of 209 species in the AniX wildlife identification dataset.
description: The Brookes Birdwing is included in the AniX training dataset. Detailed biological information will be updated in a future version.

habitat: Chalk and limestone grasslands, heathland, coastal areas
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — rock-rose (Helianthemum); Adults — small flowers
interesting_fact: The brown argus has expanded its range significantly northward in recent decades due to climate warming, colonising sites further north in Britain and Europe than it previously occupied — making it one of the 'climate change winner' species.
threats: Loss of chalk grassland, habitat fragmentation
description: The brown argus is a small butterfly, brown on both sides (unlike the common blue). Orange lunules border both wing edges, and the underside has distinctive orange and black spots on a greyish-brown background.

diet: Omnivore — fish, berries, roots, small mammals
interesting_fact: Brown bears can run at speeds up to 56 km/h despite their size, and they can consume up to 20,000 calories per day to prepare for winter hibernation.
description: The brown bear is one of the largest living carnivores on land. It has a large head with a concave facial profile, prominent shoulder hump, and long front claws used for digging roots and catching fish.

diet: Frugivore/Omnivore — fruits, berries, insects
interesting_fact: The brown-headed barbet makes its nest by excavating a hole in a dead tree branch. It produces a loud, repetitive 'kutroo-kutroo' call often described as similar to a water pump, which can be heard for several hundred metres.
description: The brown-headed barbet is a plump, green bird with a brown streaked head and throat, bright orange patches around its eyes and bill, and a heavy, pale yellow bill with bristles at the base — typical of barbets.

habitat: Tropical and subtropical forests, forest edges
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Ruellia; Adults — rotting fruit, nectar, bird droppings
geographic_range: Mexico through Central America to Peru and Bolivia
interesting_fact: Siproeta butterflies are known for their habit of following army ant swarms to feed on other insects flushed out by the ants — a behaviour called 'army ant following' that is more commonly associated with birds.
description: The brown siproeta is a medium-large butterfly with warm brown wings patterned with white and pale yellow patches. It has a distinctively broad, rounded wing shape and a graceful, gliding flight style.

diet: Herbivore — grasses, sedges
interesting_fact: African buffalo are known for their remarkable memory and group cohesion — the herd will collectively vote on which direction to move by standing and facing the desired direction.
description: The African buffalo, or Cape buffalo, is a large sub-Saharan African bovine. It has a fused, helmet-like horn boss that distinguishes it from other bovids. It is considered one of Africa's most dangerous animals.

diet: Herbivore — grasses, hay, grain
interesting_fact: Domestic cattle were domesticated from the extinct aurochs around 10,000 years ago in the Near East and independently in India and Africa.
description: A bull is an intact adult male domestic bovine. Bulls are heavily muscled with thick necks and broad foreheads. They are used in agriculture for draft work and in selective breeding.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — plants; Adults — nectar, fruit, salts, minerals
interesting_fact: Butterflies taste with their feet — they have chemoreceptors on their tarsi (feet) that detect sugar and other chemicals when they land on a leaf or flower. This helps them quickly assess whether a plant is a suitable food source or egg-laying site.
description: Butterflies are a large group of insects in the order Lepidoptera distinguished by scaled wings and diurnal activity. They undergo complete metamorphosis through four stages: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, and adult.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — cabbage, broccoli, mustard plants; Adults — nectar
geographic_range: Originally Europe and Asia; now worldwide except Antarctica (invasive)
interesting_fact: The small white butterfly is one of the most successful insects in the world, having spread to every inhabited continent from its native Europe. It was accidentally introduced to Australia in 1929 and North America in the 1860s via transport of its host plants.
description: The small white (cabbage white) butterfly has off-white wings with dark wing tips and (in females) two dark spots on the forewing. Its larvae are agricultural pests of brassica crops. It is among the most common butterflies globally.

habitat: Tropical rainforests of North Queensland, Australia
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Aristolochia vines; Adults — nectar from flowers
interesting_fact: The Cairns birdwing is Australia's largest butterfly with a wingspan up to 17 cm. It is legally protected in Australia. Planting its host vine (Aristolochia) in gardens has helped support urban populations in Cairns and surroundings.
description: The male Cairns birdwing is one of the world's most striking butterflies, with brilliant metallic green, yellow and black wings. Females are larger but less colourful, with black and white-spotted wings.

diet: Herbivore — dry grasses, shrubs, thorny plants
geographic_range: Middle East, North Africa, South Asia (domesticated)
interesting_fact: A camel's hump stores up to 36 kg of fat — not water — which can be metabolised for energy and water when food and drink are scarce.
description: The dromedary or Arabian camel has a single hump and is one of the most recognisable animals on Earth. It is adapted to extreme desert environments with the ability to endure long periods without food or water.

diet: Herbivore — seeds, buds, berries
interesting_fact: Canaries were used in coal mines as early warning systems for poisonous gases — particularly carbon monoxide. Because they are more sensitive to toxic fumes, miners could escape before being affected themselves.
description: The domestic canary was developed from the wild Atlantic canary. It is a small, active finch. The singing ability and bright yellow colour (in many domestic breeds) make it one of the most popular cage birds worldwide.

diet: Carnivore — rodents, birds, antelopes, hares
geographic_range: Africa, Middle East, Central Asia, India
interesting_fact: Caracals can leap up to 3 metres into the air to catch birds in flight, swiping at multiple birds with their paws in a single jump.
threats: Habitat loss, persecution by livestock farmers
description: The caracal is a medium-sized wild cat known for its distinctive long, black-tipped tufted ears. Its coat is uniformly reddish-tan or sandy, and it is the fastest cat of its size in Africa.

diet: Carnivore — meat, fish, small prey
interesting_fact: Cats can make around 100 different sounds; dogs can make only about 10. Cats also have a specialised collarbone that allows them to always land on their feet.
description: The domestic cat is a small carnivorous mammal and one of the most popular pet animals worldwide. It has retractable claws, binocular vision, and a flexible spine — characteristics of a natural predator.

diet: Herbivore — leaves, flowers, fruits, occasionally other insects
interesting_fact: When a caterpillar pupates, it doesn't simply reorganise — it essentially dissolves most of its body into a soup of cells, then rebuilds the butterfly or moth from specialised imaginal discs. Remarkably, the adult retains some memories from the caterpillar stage.
description: Caterpillars are the larval stage of moths and butterflies. They have a cylindrical, segmented body with three pairs of true legs and up to five pairs of prolegs. Many are cryptically coloured; others are brightly coloured to warn of toxicity.

diet: Carnivore — insects, frogs, small lizards, rodents
geographic_range: Nearly worldwide — one of the most successful birds on Earth
interesting_fact: Cattle egrets follow large mammals and farm machinery, catching insects, frogs, and other small animals disturbed by their movement. Each egret may eat 50% more food this way than by foraging alone.
description: The cattle egret is a small, stocky white heron with a short yellow bill and dark legs. During breeding season, it develops orange-buff plumes on the crown, back and chest. It is one of the few herons that has expanded its range globally.

habitat: Tropical rainforests, under rocks, logs, leaf litter
diet: Carnivore — insects, spiders, lizards, frogs, small rodents, bats
interesting_fact: The Amazonian giant centipede can catch and kill bats by climbing cave walls and ceiling, hanging suspended by its rear legs, and seizing bats mid-flight — one of the most remarkable hunting behaviours documented in an invertebrate.
description: The Amazonian giant centipede is the world's largest centipede, reaching up to 30 cm in length. It has a dark, slightly iridescent body, yellow legs, and a venomous bite powerful enough to cause significant pain in humans.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — horseshoe vetch; Adults — small flowers
interesting_fact: Chalk hill blue caterpillars are tended by ants that feed on a sugary secretion from a special gland on the caterpillar's back. In return, the ants protect the caterpillars from parasitoids and predators — a classic mutualistic relationship.
threats: Loss of chalk grassland, reduction of grazing
description: The chalk hill blue is a medium-sized butterfly. Males have silvery-pale blue upperside wings with a distinctive dark brown border; females are dark brown with orange lunules. Found almost exclusively on chalk and limestone hillsides.

habitat: Ancient woodland rides, moorland edges with tall grasses
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — grasses (especially purple moor grass); Adults — nectar from bugle and thistle
geographic_range: Europe, Northern Asia; became extinct in England (reintroduced 2018)
interesting_fact: The chequered skipper became extinct in England in 1976 due to changes in woodland management. In 2018, it was successfully reintroduced from Belgium — a pioneering conservation project. It remains present in Scotland.
threats: Loss of traditional woodland management, loss of open rides
description: The chequered skipper is a small, compact butterfly with dark brown wings chequered with pale yellow spots. It holds its wings at a characteristic angle when resting, with forewings and hindwings at different angles.

diet: Carnivore — gazelles, impalas, hares, wildebeest calves
geographic_range: Sub-Saharan Africa, small population in Iran
interesting_fact: The cheetah is the fastest land animal, capable of accelerating from 0 to 100 km/h in just 3 seconds during short bursts of speed.
threats: Habitat loss, prey depletion, human conflict, illegal pet trade
description: The cheetah has a slender, aerodynamic body with a deep chest, spotted coat, small rounded head, and distinctive black tear marks from eye to mouth. Its semi-retractable claws provide grip for high-speed chases.

diet: Unknown
interesting_fact: The Chestnut is one of 209 species in the AniX wildlife identification dataset.
description: The Chestnut is included in the AniX training dataset. Detailed biological information will be updated in a future version.

diet: Unknown
interesting_fact: The Chicken is one of 209 species in the AniX wildlife identification dataset.
description: The Chicken is included in the AniX training dataset. Detailed biological information will be updated in a future version.

diet: Omnivore — fruit, leaves, insects, small mammals
interesting_fact: Chimpanzees share approximately 98.7% of their DNA with humans and are our closest living relatives. They use tools — twigs, rocks, leaves — and pass this knowledge to younger generations.
threats: Deforestation, bushmeat hunting, disease, illegal trade
description: The common chimpanzee is a great ape with a black coat, bare face, hands, and feet. Highly intelligent, social, and communicative, they live in communities of up to 150 individuals with complex hierarchical structures.

diet: Herbivore — grass, seeds, plants, bark
geographic_range: Andes mountains of South America (Chile, Bolivia, Peru)
interesting_fact: Chinchillas have the softest fur of any land animal — with up to 60 hairs per follicle (humans have 1–4). They clean themselves by rolling in volcanic dust baths.
description: The chinchilla is a small rodent native to the Andes. It has very soft, dense silver-grey fur and large ears. Wild populations are critically reduced due to fur trade hunting.

habitat: Grasslands, coastal dunes, roadsides, waste ground with ragwort
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — ragwort (Senecio); Adults — little or nothing
geographic_range: Europe, introduced to Australia, New Zealand, and North America for ragwort control
interesting_fact: Cinnabar moth caterpillars — boldly striped in orange and black — sequester the toxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids from ragwort, making themselves poisonous to birds. The bright warning colours of both the caterpillar and moth advertise this toxicity.
threats: Loss of rough grassland, ragwort removal
description: The cinnabar moth has strikingly contrasting red and black wings — the forewing is dark grey-black with two crimson spots and a red streak; the hindwing is brilliant red bordered in black. Both adults and larvae are unpalatable to predators.

habitat: Gardens, meadows, forest edges with flowers
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — viburnum, snowberry; Adults — nectar (like a hummingbird)
interesting_fact: Clearwing moths are remarkable hummingbird mimics — they hover in front of flowers, beat their wings at 30–70 times per second (producing a hum), and even resemble hummingbirds in size and flight style. Most people mistake them for birds on first sighting.
description: The hummingbird clearwing is a hawk moth with clear (transparent) windows in its wings that lose their scales on its first flight, olive-green and burgundy colouring on its body, and a rapid hover flight while feeding on deep flowers.

habitat: Woodland edges, scrubland, garrigue in Mediterranean regions
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — buckthorn; Adults — nectar from flowers
lifespan_wild: Up to 1 year (longest-lived adult butterfly in Europe)
geographic_range: Mediterranean Europe, Canary Islands, North Africa
interesting_fact: The Cleopatra butterfly can live for up to 12 months as an adult — one of the longest adult lifespans of any European butterfly. It overwinters as an adult by entering torpor in vegetation, appearing dead until spring warmth revives it.
description: The Cleopatra resembles a brilliant version of the brimstone butterfly. Males have vivid orange-yellow wings with a deep orange central flush; females are pale greenish-white. At rest with wings closed, it is perfectly camouflaged as a leaf.

habitat: Mountain meadows, forest openings, alpine zones
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — bleeding heart (Dicentra); Adults — nectar from alpine flowers
interesting_fact: Like other Parnassius species, after mating the male glues a waxy sphragis (mating plug) to the female's abdomen that prevents further mating, ensuring paternity. The plug is so distinctive that entomologists can identify the male species by the plug's shape.
description: The Clodius parnassian is a large, white butterfly with semi-transparent wings marked with two red spots and two black spots. Its wings have a satiny, powdery texture. It is a high-altitude specialist of western mountain ranges.

diet: Carnivore — deer, pigs, monkeys, birds, porcupines
geographic_range: Himalayan foothills through mainland Southeast Asia to China
interesting_fact: The clouded leopard has the longest canine teeth relative to body size of any living cat, earning it the nickname 'modern-day sabre-toothed cat'.
threats: Deforestation, poaching for fur and bones
description: The clouded leopard is a medium-sized wild cat with a distinctive cloud-patterned coat — large dark blotches with lighter centres on a tawny background. It is an excellent climber, able to descend trees headfirst.

habitat: Open fields, meadows, clover fields, roadsides
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — clover, alfalfa, vetch; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: Clouded sulphur butterflies can see ultraviolet light, which reveals hidden patterns on the wings of potential mates invisible to humans. This UV patterning plays an important role in mate selection and species recognition.
threats: Minimal — common; pesticide use on host plant fields
description: The clouded sulphur is a medium-sized butterfly. Males are bright yellow with black wing borders; females are yellow or white (a common white form). The underside shows two silver-centred spots. It is one of the most common North American butterflies.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — wild mango trees; Adults — cannot eat
interesting_fact: The Madagascar comet moth has some of the longest hindwing tails of any moth — up to 20 cm. These tails are thought to deflect bat echolocation away from the body and may also improve aerodynamic control during flight.
description: The comet moth is considered one of the world's most spectacular moths, with a wingspan of up to 18 cm and brilliant yellow-orange wings decorated with four eyespots and dramatically elongated hindwing tails.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Derris and other legumes; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: Awl skippers are distinguished from true skippers by their larger size and rapid, strong flight — like an awl (a pointed tool) piercing the air. The common banded awl is one of the fastest-flying butterflies in its range.
description: The common banded awl is a large skipper butterfly, dark brown above with a curved pale band across the forewing. It has a rapid, powerful flight and typically rests with wings held flat open.

diet: Carnivore — small fish, aquatic invertebrates
interesting_fact: The kingfisher's bill is adapted to enter water with minimal splash — at a specific angle that avoids refraction. The Japanese Shinkansen bullet train was redesigned after the kingfisher's beak to reduce sonic booms in tunnels.
threats: Water pollution, habitat disturbance, cold winters reducing fish access
description: The common kingfisher is a small, stocky bird with dazzling electric-blue upperparts, orange-red underparts, and a long, dagger-like bill. Despite its vivid colours it is surprisingly difficult to spot, perching motionlessly above water.

habitat: Open woodland, cultivated areas, gardens, urban areas
diet: Omnivore — insects, fruit, nectar, scraps
geographic_range: South and Southeast Asia; introduced worldwide
interesting_fact: The common myna is listed among the world's 100 worst invasive alien species. It is an aggressive competitor for nest hollows and has driven native birds out of large areas in Australia, South Africa, and many island nations.
threats: Not threatened — invasive; threatens native birds in introduced regions
description: The common myna is a dark brown bird with a black hooded head, yellow eye patch, bill, and legs, and distinctive white wing patches visible in flight. It is noisy, bold, and highly adaptable, thriving in urban environments.

habitat: Open forests, scrublands, gardens, farmland with hedges
diet: Herbivore/Omnivore — seeds, buds, berries, insects
interesting_fact: Male common rosefinches are among the most variable songbirds — they are born looking like females (brown) and only develop their red plumage after their first year. The intensity of red signals health and foraging ability.
threats: Habitat changes affecting breeding and foraging sites
description: The common rosefinch is a small finch. Adult males have a distinctive crimson-red head, breast, and rump contrasting with a brownish back and wings. Females are streaked brown throughout, making them inconspicuous.

habitat: Scrublands, gardens, forest edges, urban parks
diet: Insectivore — insects, spiders, small invertebrates
interesting_fact: The tailorbird sews its nest by piercing leaf edges with its bill and threading plant fibres or spider silk through the holes to stitch two or more large leaves into a cone shape — one of the most sophisticated nest-building strategies.
description: The common tailorbird is a tiny, active warbler-type bird with bright rufous (reddish-brown) cap, olive-green upperparts, and white underparts. Males have elongated central tail feathers in breeding season.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — grasses; Adults — rotting fruit, nectar, sap
interesting_fact: Common wood nymphs vary dramatically in appearance across their range — northern populations have large eyespots and yellow patches, while southern populations may have smaller eyespots or none at all. This geographic variation led early entomologists to describe multiple species that were actually one.
description: The common wood nymph is a medium-large brown butterfly with two prominent yellow-ringed black eyespots on the forewing. Its flight is bouncing and erratic, and it often rests on tree trunks where it blends remarkably well.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various flowering plants; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: Hairstreak butterflies like the copper tail have false 'head' markings on their hindwings — eyespots and hair-like tails that mimic antennae. They rotate their hindwings when resting to animate the fake head, directing predator attacks away from their real head.
description: The copper tail is a small butterfly with coppery-orange to brown upperside wings and a distinctive orange and white tailed hindwing underside with metallic markings. The false head markings on the hindwing are characteristic of the hairstreak group.

diet: Frugivore/Omnivore — fruit, berries, insects
interesting_fact: The coppersmith barbet gets its name from its call — a repetitive 'tuk tuk tuk' sound resembling the striking of a coppersmith's hammer on metal. It can call thousands of times in succession without apparent rest.
description: The coppersmith barbet is a plump, colourful bird with a crimson forehead and throat patch, yellow eye patches and throat border, and green-streaked underparts. It is commonly found in gardens across South Asia.

diet: Herbivore — grasses, hay, grain
interesting_fact: Cows have almost 360-degree panoramic vision due to the placement of their eyes, but have poor depth perception and difficulty seeing directly ahead.
description: The domestic cow is a large domesticated bovine. It is one of the most common large animals on Earth, raised globally for milk, meat, and labour. Cows are social animals that form strong bonds within herds.

habitat: Intertidal zones, sublittoral rock, sandy and muddy seabeds
diet: Omnivore — molluscs, worms, crustaceans, carrion
geographic_range: Northeast Atlantic, from Norway to West Africa
interesting_fact: Crabs are capable of regenerating lost limbs — if a claw is torn off by a predator, it will regrow at the next moult. The new limb starts small and grows to full size over several moulting cycles.
description: The edible crab has a broad, reddish-brown shell (carapace) with a characteristic 'pie-crust' edge. It has large, powerful claws — the right is typically larger than the left. It is one of the most commercially important crabs in Europe.

habitat: Open fields, meadows, roadsides, gardens with asters
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — asters; Adults — nectar from small flowers
interesting_fact: Pearl crescent butterflies show strong sexual dimorphism in their behaviour — males perch on low vegetation and aggressively chase any passing butterfly (or even other insects) that enters their territory. Females are more secretive.
description: The pearl crescent is a small orange and black butterfly. The underside of the hindwing has a pale crescent-shaped marking on its outer edge — giving the butterfly its common name. It is a common and familiar North American butterfly.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Odontonema and related plants; Adults — nectar
geographic_range: Texas south through Central America to Peru
interesting_fact: Crimson patch butterfly females lay their eggs in large clusters on the underside of leaves, and the larvae live gregariously in masses, feeding communally before dispersing as they mature. This communal defence deters many predators.
description: The crimson patch is a striking black butterfly with a large, brilliant red-orange patch across the centre of each forewing and a yellow-spotted black hindwing. Its vivid pattern serves as aposematic warning.

diet: Carnivore — fish, mammals, birds, reptiles
interesting_fact: Crocodiles have one of the strongest bite forces on Earth — up to 22,000 Newtons — yet the muscles that open the jaw are so weak that a rubber band can hold them shut.
description: The Nile crocodile is the largest freshwater predator in Africa. It has a long, powerful tail, four short legs, armoured skin with bony scutes, and jaws lined with conical teeth for gripping prey.

habitat: Open areas, gardens, coastal scrub, forest margins
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — purslane, Portulaca; Adults — nectar, rotting fruit
geographic_range: Africa, South and Southeast Asia, Pacific islands
interesting_fact: The danaid eggfly is a remarkable case of sexual mimicry — females mimic the toxic plain tiger butterfly (Danaus chrysippus) and receive protection from predators without being toxic themselves. Males look completely different, with black wings and white spots.
description: The danaid eggfly shows extreme sexual dimorphism. Males are glossy black with large white spots ringed in iridescent purple. Females are orange and black, closely mimicking the unpalatable plain tiger butterfly.

diet: Herbivore — grasses, leaves, bark, berries
interesting_fact: Male deer (stags or bucks) grow and shed antlers annually — the fastest growing tissue in the animal kingdom, growing up to 2.5 cm per day.
description: Deer are ruminant mammals in the family Cervidae. Most male deer grow antlers. They are characterised by their slender legs, short tails, and large eyes. Over 40 species exist across diverse habitats worldwide.

diet: Omnivore — meat, plant matter
interesting_fact: Dogs were the first animals to be domesticated by humans, approximately 15,000–40,000 years ago from grey wolves. They have co-evolved with humans and can read human social cues.
description: The domestic dog is a domesticated descendant of the wolf. With over 400 recognized breeds, dogs vary enormously in size, shape, and coat. They are highly social pack animals that have formed close bonds with humans.

diet: Carnivore — fish, squid, crustaceans
geographic_range: Worldwide in tropical and temperate oceans
interesting_fact: Dolphins sleep with one eye open and one brain hemisphere active at a time — a state called unihemispheric slow-wave sleep — allowing them to keep breathing consciously.
description: The common dolphin is a small cetacean with a sleek, streamlined body, distinctive hourglass colouring on its sides, and a long, narrow beak. It is highly social, living in groups (superpods) of thousands.

diet: Herbivore — grasses, hay, shrubs
interesting_fact: Donkeys have an excellent memory and can recognise other donkeys they met up to 25 years earlier. They are also known to be more cautious than horses, refusing to put themselves in danger.
description: The domestic donkey is a member of the horse family. It has long ears, a short mane, and a thick hide resistant to harsh weather. Used for centuries as a working animal, it is renowned for its stubbornness and endurance.

diet: Carnivore — mosquitoes, flies, gnats, other insects
interesting_fact: Dragonflies are the most effective hunters in the animal kingdom — they catch up to 95% of their prey attempts. They track individual prey in a swarm, calculate the prey's trajectory, and intercept it — rather than chasing it — using a unique predictive flight strategy.
threats: Wetland loss, water pollution, pesticide use
description: The broad-bodied chaser is a stocky dragonfly. Males have a powder-blue abdomen; females and immatures have a yellow-sided brown abdomen. It is an aggressive, territorial hunter typically seen perching beside still water.

diet: Omnivore — aquatic plants, seeds, insects, molluscs, fish
geographic_range: Nearly worldwide (introduced to Australia, New Zealand, South America)
interesting_fact: A duck's quack doesn't echo — this is actually a myth. Scientists have recorded and analysed duck quacks and confirmed they do produce echoes, but the sound fades at both ends, making it difficult to hear in natural settings.
threats: Habitat loss, hunting, lead poisoning from shot
description: The mallard is the most familiar and widespread dabbling duck. Males have an iridescent green head, yellow bill, chestnut chest, and a distinctive curled tail feather; females are mottled brown with orange bills.

diet: Carnivore — rabbits, hares, squirrels, fish, birds
geographic_range: North America, Europe, Asia, North Africa
interesting_fact: The golden eagle can dive at speeds exceeding 300 km/h when hunting, and its eyesight is four to eight times more powerful than a human's.
threats: Habitat loss, electrocution from power lines, poisoning
description: The golden eagle is one of the most widely distributed eagles on Earth. It has dark brown plumage with a golden-brown crown and nape. It is a powerful predator known for its agility and speed in flight.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — nettles, elm, hops; Adults — sap, rotting fruit, nectar
interesting_fact: The comma and related angle-wing butterflies (Polygonia) have ragged, irregular wing edges that perfectly mimic dead leaves — complete with apparent holes and bite marks. The underside of the wing is cryptically leaf-brown; the tiny silver 'comma' on the hindwing is the only distinguishing mark.
description: The eastern comma is a medium butterfly with orange upperwings marked with black spots and angular, deeply scalloped wing edges. The underside is cryptic brown with a small silver comma-shaped mark on the hindwing.

habitat: Meadows, scrubland, rocky slopes with crucifers
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — wild mustard, garlic mustard; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: Marbled whites and dapple whites have intricate green mosaic patterns on their wing undersides that are completely invisible from above. When they close their wings at rest in grass or flowers, they become virtually invisible.
description: The eastern dappled white is a small butterfly with white wings above, marked with black spots on the forewing tip. The hindwing underside is beautifully marbled with green and white, providing perfect camouflage among vegetation.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — young pine needles; Adults — blueberry and other spring flowers
interesting_fact: Eastern pine elfin caterpillars feed on young pine needles and are camouflaged to match pine needles exactly in colour, texture, and orientation — lying along needles rather than across them. Even their droppings resemble pine debris.
threats: Loss of young pine stands, fire suppression
description: The eastern pine elfin is a small butterfly with irregular brownish-grey wings patterned with darker bands and scalloped edges. The patterning is intricate, providing camouflage against pine bark. It flies only briefly in early spring.

habitat: Open forests, forest edges, gardens, scrubland
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various plants; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: Pierrots are named after the white-faced Commedia dell'arte character Pierrot, because their black-on-white wing pattern resembles his costume. They are known for resting with wings flat open — unlike most hairstreak relatives.
description: The elbowed pierrot is a small butterfly, white above with a black costal border and distinctive bent (elbowed) black marking at the forewing apex. The underside has bold black spots on a white background.

diet: Herbivore — grass, fruit, bark, roots
interesting_fact: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. They also grieve their dead — examining bones and tusks of deceased herd members, sometimes standing silently for hours.
threats: Ivory poaching, habitat loss, human conflict
description: The African bush elephant is the largest living land animal. It has a long prehensile trunk used for breathing, smelling, drinking, and grasping, plus large fan-shaped ears that help regulate body temperature.

habitat: Eucalyptus forests and woodlands of Australia
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — eucalyptus, pepper trees; Adults — cannot eat
interesting_fact: The emperor gum moth is one of Australia's largest and most spectacular moths. Like all saturniid moths, adults have vestigial mouthparts and cannot eat. They survive their adult life entirely on fat reserves accumulated as caterpillars and typically live only 1–2 weeks.
description: The emperor gum moth is a large moth with a wingspan up to 15 cm. Its wings are red-brown with a distinctive eyespot on each wing, a white-edged pink and black marginal band, and a pale, pinkish-white forewing costal region.

diet: Carnivore — birds, bats, small mammals
interesting_fact: The peregrine falcon is the fastest animal on Earth, reaching speeds exceeding 390 km/h in a hunting dive (stoop) — faster than a Formula 1 racing car.
description: The peregrine falcon has a blue-grey back, barred white underparts, and a distinctive black hood. It is a master aerial predator, striking prey mid-air after breathtaking high-speed dives.

diet: Carnivore — rodents, rabbits, birds
interesting_fact: Ferrets can sleep so deeply that they appear dead and cannot be woken — a state known as 'ferret dead sleep'. They also communicate happiness by performing a bouncy dance called the 'weasel war dance'.
description: The domestic ferret is a small, elongated carnivore with a long tail, short legs, and characteristic musk glands. Despite their small size, ferrets are curious, playful, and highly energetic.

diet: Varies — carnivore, herbivore, omnivore
interesting_fact: Fish have been on Earth for over 500 million years — predating the dinosaurs by 300 million years. Some species, like the Greenland shark, can live for over 400 years.
threats: Overfishing, pollution, climate change, habitat destruction
description: Fish are aquatic vertebrates with gills, fins, and (usually) scales. They are the most species-rich group of vertebrates with over 33,000 known species occupying virtually every aquatic environment on Earth.

habitat: Shallow saline lakes, lagoons, mangroves, tidal flats
diet: Filter feeder — algae, brine shrimp, insects
geographic_range: Africa, southern Europe, Middle East, South Asia
interesting_fact: Flamingos get their pink colour from carotenoid pigments in their food. A flamingo fed a white diet would gradually become white. Flamingos in zoos are fed specially supplemented feed.
threats: Habitat loss, water pollution, human disturbance
description: The greater flamingo is the largest and most widespread flamingo species. It has pale pink plumage, a distinctive downward-bent bill, and extraordinarily long legs. They often stand on one leg to conserve body heat.

habitat: Deciduous and evergreen forests, forest edges
diet: Insectivore — insects, invertebrates
geographic_range: East and South Asia, wintering in Southeast Asia and India
interesting_fact: Unlike all other wagtails that wag their tail up and down, the forest wagtail wags its tail from side to side — an unusual behaviour that is unique within its family and may help flush insects from the forest floor.
description: The forest wagtail is an olive-grey and white bird with two distinctive dark breast bands and a pink-buff bill. It is often seen walking on forest floors, swaying its tail sideways as it searches for insects.

habitat: Forests, grasslands, mountains, deserts, urban areas
diet: Omnivore — rodents, rabbits, birds, fruits, insects
geographic_range: Widest range of any carnivore — worldwide
interesting_fact: Red foxes are highly adaptable and have colonised every continent except Antarctica. Urban foxes in cities have larger brains than their rural counterparts, an adaptation to navigating complex environments.
description: The red fox is the largest true fox with a distinctive rusty-red coat, white underparts, and a bushy white-tipped tail. It is highly intelligent, omnivorous, and exceptionally adaptable to diverse environments.

habitat: Ponds, lakes, rivers, damp forests, meadows
diet: Carnivore — insects, worms, slugs, small fish
interesting_fact: Frogs absorb water through their skin rather than drinking it. Their skin is so permeable that toxic chemicals in their environment can quickly affect them, making frogs important environmental indicators.
threats: Habitat loss, pollution, climate change, disease (chytridiomycosis)
description: The common frog is a widespread European amphibian with moist, smooth skin that ranges in colour from brown and grey to yellow-green. It hibernates underwater in winter and congregates at ponds to breed in early spring.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — wide range of plants; Adults — minimal
interesting_fact: The garden tiger moth has declined by 89% in the UK since 1968 — one of the most dramatic declines of any British moth. Climate change (milder, wetter winters) is thought to be the primary driver, as its hairy caterpillars are vulnerable to wet conditions.
threats: Climate change, habitat loss, light pollution
description: The garden tiger moth is one of the most distinctive British moths — creamy-white forewings boldly patterned with dark brown patches, and brilliant orange-red hindwings with large blue-centred black spots. The colours warn of foul taste.

habitat: Gardens, roadsides, forest edges across eastern North America
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — dandelion, plantain, sunflower; Adults — minimal
interesting_fact: The giant leopard moth caterpillar (woolly bear) curls into a ball when threatened, exposing its brilliant red abdomen between its black body rings — warning predators that it is toxic. Adults are entirely white with hollow black spots, producing a different warning signal.
description: The giant leopard moth is a large, striking white moth with bold, hollow black spots and rings on its wings, and an iridescent blue-black body with red and orange markings. Its wings span up to 9 cm.

diet: Herbivore — leaves (especially acacia), flowers, fruits
interesting_fact: A giraffe's neck is approximately 1.8 metres long but contains only seven vertebrae — the same number as humans. Its tongue is around 45 cm long and is bluish-purple, possibly to prevent sunburn.
description: The giraffe is the world's tallest living terrestrial animal. Its distinctive feature is its exceptionally long neck and legs. It has an irregular, patchwork coat pattern that is unique to each individual.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various plants; Adults — nectar, minerals
geographic_range: Texas through Central America to South America
interesting_fact: Metal mark butterflies (family Riodinidae) are named for the metallic-looking spots on their wings. The glittering sapphire produces vivid iridescent blue colouration through structural colour — microscopic structures that refract light, similar to the blue morpho.
description: The glittering sapphire is a small butterfly with brilliant iridescent blue wings in males (females are less vivid) with dark brown borders. The metallic shimmer changes intensity with viewing angle, making it one of the most dazzling small butterflies.

diet: Herbivore — leaves, stems, shrubs, grains
interesting_fact: Goats have rectangular pupils that give them a 320-degree field of view without moving their heads, compared to about 185 degrees for humans — ideal for watching for predators while grazing.
description: The domestic goat is a subspecies of the wild goat. It is one of the earliest domesticated animals. Goats are agile, curious, and intelligent, with both sexes potentially bearing horns and wattles.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various plants; Adults — rotting fruit, sap
interesting_fact: Gold-banded butterflies are typically found in dark forest understorey environments where shafts of light illuminate the forest floor. Males are territorial and perch on leaves in patches of light, chasing other males that enter their territory.
description: The gold-banded butterfly is a dark butterfly with a distinctive broad, bright yellow-gold band crossing its wings. It has a slow, flapping flight through dark forest understorey and rests with wings outspread.

diet: Omnivore — algae, insects, crustaceans, plant matter
interesting_fact: Goldfish have a memory span of at least three months — the '3-second memory' is a myth. They can recognise their owners, learn simple tasks, and remember them months later.
threats: Minimal — domesticated; wild populations threatened by invasives
description: The goldfish is one of the earliest fish to be domesticated. Originally a grey-green wild carp from East Asia, centuries of selective breeding have produced hundreds of varieties in a rainbow of colours and body shapes.

diet: Herbivore — grass, grain, aquatic plants
interesting_fact: Geese mate for life and form strong pair bonds. If one partner dies, the surviving goose may mourn and remain alone for an extended period before choosing a new partner.
description: The greylag goose is the ancestor of most domestic geese. It has a large orange bill, pale orange-pink legs, and grey-brown plumage with a white belly. It forms large, noisy flocks and migrates seasonally.

diet: Herbivore — leaves, stems, fruit, shoots, bark
interesting_fact: Gorillas are highly intelligent great apes that build nests each night to sleep in. They have been taught sign language in captivity and can communicate using hundreds of signs.
description: The western gorilla is the world's largest living primate. It has a stocky body, broad chest, and strong arms longer than its legs. Adult males develop a silver-grey back (silverbacks) as they mature.

diet: Herbivore — grasses, cereal crops, vegetables
interesting_fact: Migratory locusts can form swarms of billions that darken the sky for hours. A 1-km² swarm contains around 80 million individuals that eat 35,000 people's worth of food daily. Desert locust swarms cover up to 460,000 km² and travel 130 km in a day.
threats: Pesticide use (ironically a threat to non-pest grasshopper species)
description: The migratory locust (in its solitary phase) is a typical green-brown grasshopper. Triggered by crowding, it transforms into the gregarious phase with a distinctive yellow and black pattern and forms massive, devastating swarms.

habitat: Forests, gardens, forest margins, coastal scrub
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Portulaca and various plants; Adults — nectar, fruit
geographic_range: Widespread from Madagascar through South and Southeast Asia to Pacific Islands
interesting_fact: The great eggfly is one of the world's most widespread butterflies but shows remarkable variation across its range — populations on different islands have evolved independently into distinct-looking forms, some mimicking different toxic butterflies locally.
description: Male great eggflies are spectacular — velvety black with three pairs of large white spots ringed in iridescent purple-blue. Females are highly variable, often mimicking other butterfly species. It is one of the most widespread Nymphalid butterflies.

habitat: Tropical forests of South and Southeast Asia
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Uvaria and related plants; Adults — nectar, minerals
interesting_fact: Jay swallowtail butterflies (Graphium species) are fast-flying and often found mud-puddling in large groups alongside other species. Males gather at damp soil to extract sodium and minerals essential for reproduction.
description: The great jay is a large swallowtail butterfly, dark brown-black above with vivid blue-green iridescent markings across both wings — appearing to glow in forest light. It has an elegant, rapid flight through the rainforest canopy.

habitat: Tropical forests of Central and South America
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Aristolochia vines; Adults — nectar
geographic_range: Southern Mexico through Central America to Peru and Bolivia
interesting_fact: Cattleheart butterflies sequester toxic aristolochic acids from their Aristolochia larval host plants, making themselves unpalatable to predators. Their bright colours advertise this toxicity, and they are mimicked by several non-toxic butterfly species.
description: The green-celled cattleheart is a stunning dark swallowtail. Its wings are velvety black above with brilliant green iridescent cells on the hindwing that appear to glow. Males have crimson patches on the hindwing underside.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — gorse, bilberry, broom, rock rose; Adults — flowers
geographic_range: Europe, North Africa, parts of Asia
interesting_fact: The green hairstreak is Britain's only green butterfly — and its green is structural colour, not pigment. When resting with wings closed, the vivid green undersides make it virtually invisible against green leaves, despite being a butterfly.
threats: Loss of scrub habitats, over-management, grazing pressure
description: The green hairstreak is a small butterfly. The upperside is plain brown but rarely seen as it almost always rests with wings closed, revealing the vivid iridescent green underside with a faint white hairstreak line.

habitat: Open areas, fields, gardens, roadsides, woodland edges
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — wide range of plants; Adults — many flowers
interesting_fact: The grey hairstreak has false antennae — hindwing tails with orange-capped black eyespots that mimic the butterfly's head and antennae when it rubs its hindwings together. Predators strike the decoy, allowing the butterfly to escape with its real head intact.
description: The grey hairstreak is a small butterfly, blue-grey above (in males) and grey below with a distinctive orange and blue eyespot at the base of its short hindwing tail. It is the most widespread hairstreak in North America.

diet: Omnivore — seeds, nuts, insects, vegetables
interesting_fact: Wild hamsters can run up to 8 km per night on their foraging routes. Their cheek pouches can hold up to half their body weight in food, which they carry back to their burrows for storage.
description: The Syrian or golden hamster is a small rodent with large cheek pouches, stubby legs, and a very short tail. In the wild it is solitary and nocturnal. It is one of the most popular small pets worldwide.

habitat: Coastal waters, estuaries, rocky shores, ice floes
diet: Carnivore — fish, squid, crustaceans, molluscs
geographic_range: North Atlantic and North Pacific coasts
interesting_fact: Harbor seals can sleep underwater, surfacing automatically to breathe without waking — a state called aquatic sleep. They can also dive to 300 m and hold their breath for up to 30 minutes.
description: The harbor seal is a true seal with a spotted grey to tan coat. Unlike sea lions, it has no external ear flaps. On land it moves by caterpillar-like undulations of its body, as it cannot turn its hind flippers forward.

diet: Carnivore — rodents, rabbits, snakes, small birds
interesting_fact: Red-tailed hawks are so iconic in North America that their call is typically substituted for the bald eagle in film and TV because the actual bald eagle call is considered too soft and undramatic.
threats: Habitat loss, vehicle strikes, pesticide exposure
description: The red-tailed hawk is the most common hawk in North America. It has a distinctive rusty-red tail in adults, brown upperparts, and pale underparts with a dark belly band. It is a powerful, adaptable predator.

diet: Omnivore — insects, earthworms, frogs, berries, eggs
interesting_fact: Hedgehogs have around 5,000–7,000 spines on their backs — each one a hollow hair made stiff with keratin. When threatened, they curl into a tight ball, protecting their soft underbelly completely.
threats: Road traffic, habitat fragmentation, pesticide use
description: The European hedgehog is a small, nocturnal mammal covered in sharp spines on its back and sides. Its face, underside, and legs are covered in coarse hair. It hibernates during winter in temperate regions.

habitat: Tropical rainforests of northern Australia and New Guinea
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — bleeding heart trees; Adults — cannot eat
interesting_fact: The Hercules moth is Australia's largest moth and has the greatest wing surface area of any insect — up to 36 cm² per wing. Like other giant silkmoths, adults cannot eat and devote their entire short adult life to reproduction.
description: The Hercules moth is enormous, with a wingspan up to 27 cm. Females are larger than males. The wings are grey-brown with transparent 'windows' and long, tapering hindwing tails. Males have broader, more elongated hindwing extensions than females.

diet: Herbivore — grass, fallen fruit
interesting_fact: Despite their seemingly docile appearance, hippos are responsible for more human fatalities in Africa than any other large animal. They can run at 30 km/h and have extremely large, sharp canine teeth used in combat.
threats: Poaching for ivory teeth, habitat loss, human conflict
description: The hippopotamus is the third-largest living land mammal after elephants and white rhinos. Despite looking like it should be a pachyderm relative, DNA analysis shows the hippo's closest living relatives are cetaceans — whales and dolphins.

diet: Herbivore — grass, hay, grains
interesting_fact: Horses sleep standing up using a 'stay apparatus' of ligaments and tendons to lock their knees. They only lie down for REM sleep, typically for 2–3 hours per day.
description: The domestic horse is a large, hoofed mammal that has lived alongside humans for 5,000 years. With strong, muscular bodies and great stamina, horses revolutionised transportation, agriculture, and warfare throughout history.

habitat: Open fields, gardens, scrubland with flowers
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — bedstraw; Adults — nectar from deep flowers
interesting_fact: The hummingbird hawk-moth is so named because it hovers in front of flowers while feeding — exactly like a hummingbird — beating its wings 70–85 times per second and producing a humming sound. Most observers genuinely mistake it for a hummingbird.
description: The hummingbird hawk-moth has olive-brown forewings, bright orange hindwings, and a distinctive fan-shaped tail of black-and-white tufts. Its proboscis can be as long as its entire body, allowing it to reach deep into long flowers.

diet: Carnivore/scavenger — wildebeest, zebra, carrion
interesting_fact: Spotted hyenas are not primarily scavengers — they kill up to 95% of their food. They are also the only mammals with a pseudo-penis in the female, making sex determination challenging without expert examination.
threats: Habitat loss, persecution by livestock farmers, snaring
description: The spotted hyena, also called the laughing hyena, is the largest and most abundant hyena species. It has a powerful build, large rounded ears, spots on its coat, and a sloping back due to longer front legs.

habitat: Tropical rainforests, dry forests, coastal areas
diet: Herbivore — leaves, fruits, flowers
interesting_fact: Iguanas have a third eye on top of their head called the parietal eye which detects changes in light and shadow, helping them sense predators approaching from above.
threats: Habitat loss, hunting for meat and leather, pet trade
description: The green iguana is a large, arboreal lizard with a distinctive dewlap, a row of dorsal spines from neck to tail, and rough scales. Despite being called 'green', adults can vary from bright green to brown and even orange.

habitat: Rocky cliffs, canyons, sagebrush scrub of western mountains
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — native umbellifers (carrot family); Adults — nectar
geographic_range: Rocky Mountain region of western North America
interesting_fact: The Indra swallowtail is adapted to harsh, high-elevation rocky habitats and is one of the few swallowtails that specialises in cliff-face terrain. Different isolated mountain populations show distinct wing pattern variations, making it a classic example of geographic speciation in progress.
threats: Habitat disturbance, overgrazing affecting larval host plants
description: The Indra swallowtail is a black swallowtail with pale yellow spots in bands across the wings, blue hindwing patches, and red-orange eyespots near the tail. It has short tails relative to other swallowtails.

habitat: Forests, forest edges, parks — eastern North America
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — wide variety of trees; Adults — cannot eat
interesting_fact: The io moth has enormous, false eyespots on its hindwings that are normally hidden by the cryptic forewing. When disturbed, it suddenly reveals these eyespots — simulating a large predator's eyes — often startling birds long enough for the moth to escape.
description: The io moth shows strong sexual dimorphism. Males have yellow forewings; females have reddish-brown. Both have brilliant, cow-sized eyespots on the hindwings — large blue and black concentric circles on a yellow background — used as a startle defence.

habitat: Tropical forests of Central and South America
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various tropical plants; Adults — rotting fruit, sap
interesting_fact: Sister butterflies (genus Adelpha) form one of the most species-rich butterfly genera in the Americas, with over 85 species. Many are difficult to distinguish even for experts, as they mimic each other and vary considerably across their range.
description: The Iphiclus sister is a medium-sized butterfly, dark brown-black above with a broad, bright white band sweeping across both wings, and orange hindwing markings. The pattern is typical of many Adelpha 'sister' species.

diet: Carnivore — deer, capybara, peccaries, caimans, fish
geographic_range: Central and South America, from Mexico to Argentina
interesting_fact: The jaguar has the strongest bite force of any big cat relative to its size, capable of piercing turtle shells and killing prey with a single bite through the skull. It is the only big cat in the Americas.
threats: Deforestation, prey depletion, ranching conflicts, trafficking
description: The jaguar is the largest cat in the Americas and the third largest in the world. It has a stocky, muscular build with a pale yellow coat covered in black rosettes with spots inside — distinguishing it from the leopard.

diet: Carnivore — plankton, fish eggs, small fish
interesting_fact: One species of jellyfish, Turritopsis dohrnii, is considered biologically immortal — it can revert from its adult form back to an immature polyp and restart its life cycle when stressed.
description: The moon jellyfish is a translucent, disc-shaped cnidarian with four distinctive horseshoe-shaped gonads visible through the bell. It drifts with ocean currents and uses trailing tentacles to capture small prey.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — passionflower vines; Adults — nectar, tear-drinking
geographic_range: Florida, Caribbean, Central and South America
interesting_fact: Julia butterflies have been documented drinking tears from the eyes of basking crocodilians and sleeping birds — a behaviour called lachryphagy. The sodium and amino acids in tears supplement minerals that are difficult to find in nectar.
description: The Julia butterfly is a large, elongated heliconian with vivid orange-flame to reddish-orange wings bordered in black. Males are more vibrant than females. Its long, narrow wing shape gives it an elegant, gliding flight.

habitat: Warm, humid tropical and subtropical regions
diet: N/A — plant
interesting_fact: Jute is the second most important natural fibre after cotton. Jute bags and sacks are biodegradable and have been used for thousands of years. Bangladesh and India produce about 95% of the world's jute.
description: Jute is a flowering plant grown primarily for its soft, shiny bast fibre. The stems grow 2–4 m tall. The fibre is extracted from the stem and used to make burlap, hessian cloth, rope, and biodegradable packaging.

diet: Herbivore — grasses, shrubs, leaves
interesting_fact: Kangaroos are the only large animals to use hopping as their primary form of locomotion. The red kangaroo can reach speeds of 70 km/h and jump over 9 metres in a single bound.
description: The red kangaroo is the largest marsupial and the largest native land animal of Australia. Females carry underdeveloped young (joeys) in a forward-facing pouch. Males have distinctive reddish fur.

diet: Herbivore — eucalyptus leaves
interesting_fact: Koala fingerprints are virtually identical to human fingerprints, even under a microscope — so similar that they have occasionally confused crime scene investigators.
threats: Habitat loss, bushfires, disease (chlamydia), vehicle strikes
description: The koala is an arboreal marsupial with a large round head, fluffy ears, and a spoon-shaped nose. It spends up to 22 hours per day sleeping to conserve energy, as eucalyptus leaves are low in nutrition.

habitat: Gardens, fields, woodlands, meadows — wherever aphids occur
diet: Carnivore — aphids, scale insects, mites
interesting_fact: A single seven-spot ladybird can eat up to 5,000 aphids in its lifetime. Ladybirds produce a foul-smelling, toxic yellow fluid from their leg joints (reflex bleeding) when threatened, deterring most predators.
threats: Invasive harlequin ladybird competition, habitat loss, pesticides
description: The seven-spot ladybird is a small, domed beetle with vivid red elytra (wing cases) bearing seven black spots — three on each wing case plus one shared spot across the centre. It is one of the most beneficial insects in gardens.

habitat: Gardens, farmlands, forests — wherever scale insects and aphids occur
diet: Carnivore — aphids, scale insects
geographic_range: Native to Asia; introduced and invasive worldwide
interesting_fact: The harlequin ladybug is the most invasive ladybug on Earth, having spread from deliberate biological control releases across Europe and North America. It outcompetes native ladybug species for prey and habitat and has been linked to declines in dozens of native ladybug populations.
description: The harlequin ladybug shows extreme colour variation — from black with red spots to red with black spots and many variations between. It is generally larger and more variable in colour than native ladybug species, making identification complex.

habitat: Open woodlands, meadows, roadsides with mustard plants
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — native mustards; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: The large marble butterfly is one of the first butterflies to emerge in spring in many western habitats. Its intricate green marbling on the hindwing underside provides near-perfect camouflage when it perches on flowerheads with wings closed.
threats: Urban development, loss of native mustard plants
description: The large marble is a medium white butterfly with black forewing patterning above. The hindwing underside is beautifully mottled with green and yellow-green, resembling mottled limestone — providing exceptional camouflage.

diet: Omnivore — fruit, leaves, flowers, insects, small animals
interesting_fact: Ring-tailed lemurs engage in 'stink fights' — males rub their tails over scent glands on their wrists and then wave their tails at rivals, wafting scent in their direction.
threats: Deforestation, hunting, live capture for pet trade
description: The ring-tailed lemur is one of the most recognisable primates, with its distinctive long tail banded with black and white rings. It is highly social and spends more time on the ground than other lemurs.

diet: Carnivore — antelopes, deer, monkeys, rodents, birds
interesting_fact: Leopards are the only big cat that regularly stores kills in trees to protect food from lions and hyenas, sometimes dragging carcasses heavier than themselves up into the branches.
threats: Habitat loss, prey decline, human persecution, illegal trade
description: The leopard is the most widespread big cat in the world. It has a pale to dark golden coat covered with black spots arranged in rosettes. It is largely solitary and nocturnal, with extraordinary adaptability.

diet: Carnivore — zebras, wildebeests, antelopes, buffalo
interesting_fact: Lions are the only cats that live in social groups (prides). A pride is typically made up of related females and their cubs, plus a small coalition of males, who protect the territory.
threats: Habitat loss, human conflict, trophy hunting, prey depletion
description: The African lion is the second-largest living cat after the tiger. Males are distinguished by their prominent mane that grows darker and fuller with age. Lions are famously the only cats where males and females look distinctly different.

diet: Omnivore — insects, spiders, worms, berries
interesting_fact: Lizards can detach their tails voluntarily when grabbed by a predator — a behaviour called autotomy. The tail wriggles to distract the predator while the lizard escapes. A new tail regenerates but is made of cartilage.
threats: Habitat loss, pesticide use, invasive species
description: The European green lizard is a medium-sized lizard. Males are bright green with a blue throat during breeding season. It basks in sunny spots and is an agile hunter of invertebrates.

diet: Unknown
interesting_fact: The Lizard Images2 is one of 209 species in the AniX wildlife identification dataset.
description: The Lizard Images2 is included in the AniX training dataset. Detailed biological information will be updated in a future version.

habitat: Deciduous forests with walnut, hickory, sweet gum, birch
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various deciduous trees; Adults — cannot eat
interesting_fact: The luna moth's long hindwing tails are thought to deflect bat echolocation. Studies found that moths with tails were substantially more likely to escape bat attacks, as the bat's sonar locks onto the rotating tail rather than the moth's body.
threats: Light pollution (disrupts navigation and mating), pesticides, habitat loss
description: The luna moth is one of North America's most spectacular insects — a large, pale lime-green moth with purple-pink borders, crescent-shaped eyespots on each wing, and dramatically elongated hindwing tails. It flies only at night for its single week of adult life.

diet: Carnivore — roe deer, hares, chamois, birds
interesting_fact: The Eurasian lynx has enormous paws that act as natural snowshoes, enabling it to walk on top of deep snow while chasing prey. Each paw is about the size of a human fist.
description: The Eurasian lynx is the largest of the lynx species. It has long legs, large padded paws, a short tail with a black tip, tufted ears, and a spotted coat that varies from yellow-brown to grey.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Omphalea vines; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: The Madagascan sunset moth is widely considered the world's most beautiful moth — and one of the most spectacular insects of any kind. Its vivid iridescent colours are structural rather than pigment-based, produced by microscopic ridged scales that diffract light.
description: The Madagascan sunset moth has wings blazing with iridescent greens, reds, blues, and oranges in complex patterns — all structural colour that shifts as the viewing angle changes. White and black markings frame the iridescent areas. Despite its brilliance, it is a moth, not a butterfly.

diet: N/A — plant
interesting_fact: Maize (corn) is the world's most produced crop by volume. It originated in Mexico approximately 10,000 years ago from a wild grass called teosinte. Modern corn has been so selectively bred that it can no longer survive or reproduce without human intervention.
description: Maize is a tall cereal plant reaching 2–3 m, with broad leaves and a distinctive ear of kernels (corn cob). It is a global staple food, used for animal feed, biofuel, and a wide range of food products. Each cob contains approximately 800 kernels in 16 rows.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Ruellia; Adults — rotting fruit, flowers, bird droppings
geographic_range: Florida, Caribbean, Central and South America
interesting_fact: The malachite butterfly is named for its resemblance to the green mineral malachite. Unlike most butterflies that feed primarily on nectar, the malachite strongly prefers rotting fruit, dung, and carrion, from which it extracts amino acids and minerals unavailable in nectar.
description: The malachite is a large butterfly with dark brown wings decorated with bold, brilliant green panels — a combination that creates a strong visual similarity to the banded mineral malachite. It is a graceful, gliding flier.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — red mangrove; Adults — nectar
geographic_range: Florida, Caribbean, Central America, South America
interesting_fact: The mangrove skipper is unusual in being completely dependent on mangrove trees — one of the most threatened coastal habitats on Earth. Larvae feed exclusively on red mangrove leaves, and adults rarely stray far from mangrove forests.
description: The mangrove skipper is a large, iridescent butterfly with brilliant blue-green sheen across its dark wings — one of the most iridescent of all skipper butterflies. It has a fast, direct flight along mangrove forest edges.

diet: Unknown
interesting_fact: This class corresponds to training data labelled 'Marino' in the dataset. The exact species identity is uncertain based on the class name alone.
description: This is a dataset class labelled 'Marino'. Species data is unavailable as this may correspond to a specific regional or colloquial name for an animal not yet identified in the database.

habitat: Open disturbed areas, roadsides, stream edges
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — noseburn (Tragia); Adults — various flowers
geographic_range: South Texas through Central America to Argentina
