interesting_fact: The common mestra is a highly nomadic butterfly, ranging much further north than its typical range during warm summer periods. It regularly colonises southern Texas in large numbers during warm years, then retreats south with cold weather.
description: The common mestra is a medium-sized, white and orange butterfly. Its wings are white above with orange-brown borders and patches, and pale peach-cream with darker veining below. It has a leisurely, drifting flight.

habitat: Streamside areas, washes, weedy fields in arid regions
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — seep willow (Baccharis); Adults — flowers
interesting_fact: Metalmark butterflies always rest with their wings spread flat open — unlike most butterflies that fold their wings above their bodies. This keeps the metallic wing spots visible, which may deter predators or aid in thermoregulation.
description: The fatal metalmark is a small butterfly with warm reddish-brown wings patterned with dark spots and fine metallic silver-grey spots near the wing margins — giving metalmarks their name. It holds its wings flat open when resting.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — nettles; Adults — nectar, sap, rotting fruit
geographic_range: North America, primarily northern regions and mountains
interesting_fact: Milbert's tortoiseshell is one of the first butterflies to emerge in spring in cold climates because it overwinters as an adult butterfly — sheltering in rock crevices, buildings, and hollow logs — and emerges to fly on warm days even in early February.
description: Milbert's tortoiseshell has dark brown wings with a vivid orange-yellow submarginal band, blue spots along the outer margins, and pale blue spots at the forewing tips. The underside is cryptically dark, providing camouflage at rest.

diet: Carnivore — earthworms, insects, grubs
interesting_fact: Moles can detect the electrical fields of earthworms through receptors in their snout called Eimer's organs. Their blood also has special haemoglobin that allows them to reuse exhaled oxygen in low-oxygen tunnels.
description: The European mole is a small, dark-grey to black mammal with a cylindrical body, velvety fur, large front paws with long claws for digging, and tiny eyes almost hidden beneath its fur. It spends most of its life underground.

habitat: Meadows, fields, marshes, roadsides — wherever milkweed grows
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — milkweed; Adults — nectar
geographic_range: North America; migrates to Mexico and California
interesting_fact: The monarch butterfly undertakes one of the most remarkable migrations on Earth — up to 4,500 km from Canada and northern US to overwintering sites in Mexico. No individual makes the full round trip; it takes 3–4 generations, but descendants navigate back to the same specific trees.
threats: Milkweed loss, climate change, deforestation of overwintering sites, pesticides
description: The monarch butterfly has vivid orange wings with black vein patterns and white-spotted black borders. Its larvae feed exclusively on milkweed, sequestering toxic cardenolides that make the butterfly distasteful to most predators.

diet: Carnivore — snakes, rodents, eggs, insects, frogs
interesting_fact: Mongooses are famous for their ability to fight and kill venomous snakes. They have mutated acetylcholine receptors that make them partially resistant to cobra venom, allowing them to survive bites that would kill other animals.
description: The Egyptian mongoose has a long body, short legs, pointed muzzle, and a bushy tail. Its coarse fur is greyish-brown with black-tipped guard hairs. It is bold, fast, and an adept predator of reptiles.

diet: Omnivore — fruit, leaves, insects, small animals
geographic_range: Africa, Asia, Central and South America
interesting_fact: Monkeys use sophisticated calls and facial expressions to communicate within social groups. Some species have been observed making and using rudimentary tools similar to, but less complex than, great ape tool use.
description: Monkeys are a large group of primates, typically distinguished from apes by having tails. Old World monkeys (Africa, Asia) tend to have non-prehensile tails; New World monkeys (Americas) often have prehensile tails for gripping.

habitat: Stagnant water, marshes, urban areas worldwide
diet: Females: blood; Males: nectar and plant sugars
interesting_fact: Mosquitoes are the deadliest animals on Earth — responsible for more human deaths than any other organism. They transmit malaria, dengue, Zika, yellow fever, and West Nile virus. However, only female mosquitoes bite; males feed exclusively on plant nectar.
threats: None — abundant; subject to control programs
description: The common house mosquito is a small, slender fly with scales on its wings and body, a long proboscis, and long, slender legs. Its wings produce the characteristic high-pitched whine. Females need a blood meal to produce eggs.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — willow, elm, birch, poplar; Adults — sap, rotting fruit, occasionally flowers
lifespan_wild: Up to 1 year (one of the longest-lived butterflies)
interesting_fact: The mourning cloak is one of the longest-lived butterflies in the world, surviving up to 10–11 months as an adult. It overwinters in tree crevices and emerges on warm winter days, often being one of the first butterflies seen in late winter.
description: The mourning cloak is a large, distinctive butterfly with dark maroon wings edged with a band of bright yellow-cream bordered by blue spots. The underside is cryptically streaked dark brown. Its angular wing shape adds to its dramatic appearance.

diet: Omnivore — seeds, grains, fruit, insects
interesting_fact: House mice are so small and flexible that they can squeeze through any gap larger than 6 mm — roughly the diameter of a pencil. They can also jump 46 cm, swim, and run along thin ropes and wires.
description: The house mouse is a small rodent with large ears, small eyes, a pointed snout, and a long hairless or sparsely haired tail. It is one of the most ubiquitous mammals on Earth, having spread worldwide by travelling with humans.

habitat: Tropical rainforests, mangroves, dry forests, scrublands
diet: Carnivore — rodents, rabbits, iguanas, fish, frogs
geographic_range: Southern US through Central and South America
interesting_fact: Ocelots are largely nocturnal and are excellent swimmers. They mark their territories with urine and have overlapping home ranges between males and females but not between same-sex individuals.
threats: Habitat loss, road kills, fur trade (historically)
description: The ocelot is a small wild cat with a tawny to grey coat marked with black-bordered brown spots and longitudinal streaks. It is primarily nocturnal and arboreal, using its superb night vision to hunt prey.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — oleander, periwinkle; Adults — nectar from deep flowers
interesting_fact: The oleander hawk-moth is one of the world's most spectacularly beautiful moths, with intricate camouflage colouring on its wings. Its larvae (which feed on toxic oleander) are equally dramatic — bright green with false eyespots that mimic a snake head.
description: The oleander hawk-moth has exquisitely detailed wing patterns blending streaks, mottles, and patches of pink, green, and white — resembling crumpled leaves or tree bark. This cryptic camouflage makes it nearly invisible when resting on foliage.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various plants; Adults — rotting fruit, sap, flowers
interesting_fact: The orange oakleaf butterfly has the most celebrated camouflage in the insect world. When wings are closed, it is indistinguishable from a dead leaf — complete with simulated midrib, veins, and even 'fungal spots', 'bite marks', and colour variation across individuals.
description: Upperside wings are brilliant blue and vivid orange with white spots — spectacular in flight. But when the butterfly lands and closes its wings, the underside reveals a perfect dead leaf simulacrum, including a petiole-like projection at the wing base.

habitat: Woodland rides, meadows, hedgerows, river banks
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — cuckoo flower, garlic mustard; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: Only male orange tips have the bright orange wingtips. Females are white with black tips, mimicking other white butterflies to avoid harassment by males of other species. Both sexes have beautiful green and white marbled underwings that camouflage them perfectly on garlic mustard flowerheads.
threats: Loss of hedgerows, over-tidying of roadsides removing host plants
description: The orange tip is a medium-sized white butterfly. Males have distinctive bright orange tips to their white forewings. The hindwing underside is marbled green and white — providing camouflage when resting among vegetation.

habitat: Forest margins, orchards, gardens of eastern Australia
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — citrus plants; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: The orchard swallowtail is one of Australia's most common large butterflies and has benefited from the spread of citrus cultivation — its larvae feed on citrus leaves, making it a minor pest in orchards while also being a beloved garden butterfly.
description: The orchard swallowtail shows strong sexual dimorphism. Males are black with white spots forming bands across the wings. Females are polymorphic with brown, orange, and white patterning that varies between individuals and mimics the toxic pipevine swallowtail.

diet: Omnivore — plants, seeds, insects, small reptiles
interesting_fact: The ostrich is the world's largest and heaviest bird, and lays the largest eggs of any living animal. Despite being flightless, it can run at 70 km/h — faster than any other bird — and sustain 50 km/h for long distances.
description: The ostrich has long, powerful legs, a long neck, and a small feathered head with large eyes — the largest eyes of any land animal. Males have distinctive black and white plumage; females are greyish-brown.

diet: Carnivore — fish, crustaceans, frogs, waterbirds
interesting_fact: Sea otters use rocks as tools to crack open shellfish, floating on their backs and balancing the shells on their chests. They also hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart from their group.
threats: Pollution, habitat loss, accidental drowning in fish traps
description: The Eurasian otter is a streamlined semiaquatic mammal with a long body, short legs, webbed feet, and a powerful tapered tail. Its dense, waterproof fur traps air to provide buoyancy and insulation in cold water.

diet: Carnivore — rodents, small birds, rabbits, insects
interesting_fact: Owls cannot move their eyeballs — their eyes are fixed in their sockets. To compensate, owls can rotate their heads up to 270 degrees, thanks to a specialised blood-pooling system that keeps the brain supplied as the neck twists.
threats: Habitat loss, rodenticide poisoning, vehicle strikes
description: The tawny owl is the most common owl in Europe. It has a rounded head without ear tufts, mottled brown plumage, and large dark eyes. Its classic 'twit-twoo' call is actually a duet between male and female.

habitat: Open habitats worldwide — fields, gardens, roadsides, meadows
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — thistles, mallows, nettles; Adults — various flowers
geographic_range: Worldwide — the most widely distributed butterfly on Earth
interesting_fact: The painted lady makes one of the longest migrations of any butterfly — up to 15,000 km from sub-Saharan Africa to the Arctic Circle and back, across multiple generations. The full round trip was only confirmed in 2021 using chemical analysis of wing scales.
threats: Minimal — extremely abundant; population fluctuates with thistle availability
description: The painted lady has orange and black patterned wings with white spots near the forewing tip. It is smaller than a monarch but similarly patterned. The underside has a beautiful mottled pattern of browns, whites, and small blue eyespots.

diet: Herbivore — bamboo (99% of diet)
geographic_range: Sichuan, Shaanxi and Gansu provinces, China
interesting_fact: Giant pandas have a thumb-like extension of their wrist bone that acts like a sixth finger, allowing them to grip bamboo stalks. A panda can eat 12–38 kg of bamboo every day.
threats: Habitat fragmentation, climate change affecting bamboo
description: The giant panda is a bear species endemic to China, easily recognised by its bold black-and-white colouring. Despite being in the order Carnivora, it eats almost exclusively bamboo and has evolved special adaptations for this fibrous diet.

habitat: Mangrove forests, lowland forests, coastal areas
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Parsonsia vines; Adults — flowers
geographic_range: Southeast Asia, Philippines, Taiwan, coastal Australia
interesting_fact: The paper kite butterfly has a remarkably slow, floating flight — beating its wings at a leisurely pace and drifting on air currents like a leaf. This behaviour is believed to be related to its unpalatability — it makes no attempt to flee predators.
description: The paper kite is a large butterfly — one of the biggest in Asia — with white wings decorated with intricate black vein patterns and black spots of varying sizes. It has an elegant, unhurried, floating flight through forest canopy.

diet: Herbivore — fruit, seeds, nuts, flowers, leaves
geographic_range: Trinidad and Tobago, northeast Venezuela, Guianas, northern Brazil
interesting_fact: Parrots can mimic human speech because they can modify their vocal tracts in a complex way similar to humans. African grey parrots have demonstrated cognitive abilities comparable to those of a 5-year-old child.
description: The orange-winged Amazon parrot is a predominantly green parrot with blue and yellow feathers on its head and orange wing speculum. It is vocal, intelligent, and forms strong social bonds with mates and flock members.

habitat: Dry and moist deciduous forests, farmlands, gardens
diet: Omnivore — seeds, insects, berries, reptiles
interesting_fact: The peacock's spectacular tail train (the fan of iridescent feathers) can be up to 1.8 m long — longer than the bird's body. The 'eyes' in the train are used to attract peahens during mating displays.
description: The Indian peafowl is a large, brightly coloured bird. The male (peacock) has iridescent blue-green plumage and an extravagant train of decorative tail feathers with eyespots. The female (peahen) is brown and less showy.

habitat: Cold coastal waters, rocky shorelines, islands
diet: Carnivore — fish, squid, krill
geographic_range: Coastal regions of Africa (Namibia, South Africa)
interesting_fact: African penguins are the only penguin species that breeds in Africa. They communicate with donkey-like brays, earning them the nickname 'jackass penguin'. Each individual has a unique chest spot pattern.
threats: Oil spills, overfishing reducing prey, climate change
description: The African penguin has black-and-white plumage with a distinctive black facial mask and a band of black spots across the white chest. Flightless in air, it is an exceptional underwater swimmer capable of reaching 20 km/h.

diet: Omnivore — roots, grain, fruit, insects, small animals
interesting_fact: Pigs are considered among the most intelligent animals on Earth, with cognitive abilities comparable to a 3-year-old child. They can learn their own name, play video games using joysticks, and solve complex puzzles.
description: The domestic pig is a medium-large omnivorous mammal descended from the wild boar. It has a barrel-shaped body, snout adapted for rooting, and sparse hair. Pigs are highly social and communicate with a complex array of grunts and squeals.

habitat: Coniferous forests, pine and fir forests of western mountains
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — pine, Douglas fir needles; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: Pine white butterflies have outbreak years when caterpillar populations explode and can defoliate large areas of pines. The adults fill the forest during such outbreaks — they can be so numerous that they appear like white snowflakes drifting through the trees.
description: The pine white is a small, white butterfly with black forewing borders and black wing veins. Females have orange hindwing underside veining. It flies high in the pine forest canopy — most easily seen from a distance as pale specks drifting through the trees.

habitat: Deciduous forests, woodland edges, fields, gardens
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — pipevine (Aristolochia); Adults — nectar, wet sand
interesting_fact: The pipevine swallowtail is one of North America's most important model species — at least six other butterfly species mimic its appearance to gain protection from predators. Its toxicity from pipevine aristolochic acids makes it genuinely distasteful.
description: The pipevine swallowtail has iridescent blue-green on its hindwing upperside and a row of orange spots on the hindwing underside. The underside of the hindwing is blue-black with orange and iridescent blue patches — one of the most striking undersides of any swallowtail.

habitat: Arctic sea ice, coastlines, open ocean
diet: Carnivore — seals, walrus, beluga whales, carrion
interesting_fact: Polar bears' fur appears white but is actually transparent — each hollow hair shaft scatters light, creating the appearance of white. Their skin underneath is black to absorb heat from the sun.
threats: Climate change (melting sea ice), pollution, overhunting (historically)
description: The polar bear is the world's largest land carnivore. Superbly adapted to the Arctic, it has thick subcutaneous fat, dense waterproof fur, and large paws that act as paddles for swimming long distances between ice floes.

habitat: Deciduous and mixed forests of North America
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — oak, birch, maple, willow; Adults — cannot eat
interesting_fact: The polyphemus moth's large eyespots are named after the one-eyed cyclops Polyphemus of Greek mythology. When disturbed, the moth reveals these spots — which resemble owl eyes — to startle predators. The spots are so convincing that even experienced naturalists are initially startled.
threats: Light pollution (male moths fly toward lights instead of toward females, disrupting mating)
description: The polyphemus moth is a large, tan-to-reddish-brown moth with a wingspan up to 15 cm. Each hindwing has a large, transparent eyespot ringed in blue, black, yellow, and pink — among the most striking of any North American moth.

habitat: Tropical forests of South and Southeast Asia
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various plants; Adults — rotting fruit, sap
interesting_fact: The popinjay is named for the popinjay (an archaic word for parrot), reflecting its bright, multicoloured wing patterns. Males are highly territorial and engage in dramatic aerial displays to defend their sunny forest clearings from rivals.
description: The popinjay is a medium-sized butterfly with dark wings decorated with vivid blue-white spots and bands. The pattern is complex and varies between subspecies across its range. It has a fast, erratic flight through the forest.

diet: Herbivore — bark, roots, tubers, fruits, bulbs
interesting_fact: Porcupines cannot shoot their quills — this is a myth. When threatened, they charge backward into predators, embedding the quills. The quills are modified hairs with microscopic barbs that anchor them into flesh.
threats: Habitat loss, hunting for bushmeat and quills
description: The African crested porcupine is the world's largest porcupine. It is covered in long, sharp black-and-white quills that it rattles as a warning. When attacked, it reverses into the predator with remarkable speed.

diet: Carnivore — deer, elk, livestock, smaller prey
geographic_range: Western North America through most of South America
interesting_fact: Pumas hold the Guinness World Record for the mammal with the most common names: cougar, mountain lion, panther, catamount — over 40 names in English alone. Despite their size, they are more closely related to small cats than to big cats and cannot roar.
description: The puma (also called cougar or mountain lion) is a large felid with an unmarked tawny coat, white underparts, and a long, rounded tail. It is the most widespread large wild terrestrial mammal in the Western Hemisphere.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — oak leaves; Adults — honeydew (from aphids)
interesting_fact: The purple hairstreak spends most of its life in the oak tree canopy and is easily missed — it rarely descends below the treetops. Unlike most butterflies, it feeds primarily on honeydew secreted by aphids rather than on nectar from flowers.
description: The purple hairstreak has deep blue-purple iridescent upperside wings (most vivid in males), and pale grey underwings with a white hairstreak line and orange eyespot. It flies around oak crowns in August, often visible only as glinting specks in the treetops.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — docks and knotweeds; Adults — various flowers
interesting_fact: Copper butterflies are named for the metallic copper sheen on their wings — created not by copper pigment but by structural colour. The purplish copper has an unusual purplish iridescence in males that changes with the viewing angle.
threats: Wetland loss, overgrazing of host plants
description: The purplish copper is a small butterfly. Males have coppery-orange to purplish upperwings with dark spots and an orange zigzag border. Females are more orange. Underwings are pale orange-grey with dark spots and an orange border.

habitat: Wooded areas, city parks, forest edges, stream corridors
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — elm, nettle, hackberry; Adults — rotting fruit, sap, dung, seldom flowers
interesting_fact: The question mark butterfly is named for the silver mark on its hindwing underside, which consists of a comma-curved mark plus a separate dot — forming a question mark (?) shape. Its close relative, the eastern comma, has only the curved mark.
description: The question mark has angular, ragged-edged orange and black wings. The underside is cryptically bark-like. There are two seasonal forms — the summer form has predominantly black hindwings; the winter form is more orange. Both have the silver question mark on the hindwing underside.

diet: Herbivore — grass, hay, vegetables, tree bark
interesting_fact: Rabbits eat their own droppings — a practice called caecotrophy. The first set of droppings (caecotropes) are nutrient-rich soft pellets that are re-ingested directly from the anus to extract maximum nutrition from their food.
threats: Myxomatosis, rabbit haemorrhagic disease, habitat loss, predators
description: The European rabbit is a small mammal with long ears, a short fluffy tail, and powerful hind legs for running and jumping. It lives in social underground burrow systems called warrens and is one of the most widely distributed mammals worldwide.

diet: Omnivore — fruits, nuts, insects, frogs, fish, human food
geographic_range: North America, introduced in Europe and Japan
interesting_fact: Raccoons have extremely dexterous front paws with four times more sensory cells than the rest of their body. They can unlock gates, open jars, and even turn doorknobs. Their 'washing' behaviour in water heightens their sense of touch.
description: The raccoon has distinctive black facial markings resembling a mask, a bushy tail with alternating black and grey rings, and greyish-brown fur. It is extraordinarily adaptable and has thrived in urban environments.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — stinging nettles; Adults — rotting fruit, ivy flowers, sap
geographic_range: Europe, North Africa, parts of Asia, North America
interesting_fact: Red admirals are famous for their tolerance of rotting fruit and are often found feeding at fallen apples in autumn, sometimes becoming noticeably intoxicated. They are very territorial males that return repeatedly to the same sunny spots.
threats: Minimal — common; some population decline in northern Europe
description: The red admiral is one of the most recognisable European butterflies, with velvety black wings crossed by vivid red-orange bands and tipped with white spots. The underside has intricate blue, black, and rust-brown patterning.

habitat: Tropical and subtropical dry forests, forest edges
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — Croton plants; Adults — rotting fruit, sap, dung
geographic_range: Texas through Central America to South America
interesting_fact: Cracker butterflies are the only butterflies known to make audible sounds. Males produce a cracking or clicking sound with their wings during territorial aerial pursuits of rivals — the mechanism of sound production has been debated for over a century and is still not fully understood.
description: The red cracker has dark bluish-grey wings intricately patterned with red, white, and black markings. At rest with wings folded on a tree trunk, it is almost perfectly camouflaged. Males perch head-down on tree trunks and defend territories.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — passionflower vines; Adults — nectar, pollen
interesting_fact: Heliconius erato and Heliconius melpomene are perhaps the world's most studied example of Müllerian mimicry — two toxic, unrelated species that have converged on identical wing patterns across their range. Across 29 geographic areas, each pair has evolved a distinct pattern convergently.
description: The red postman typically has black wings with a broad red band on the forewing and yellow on the hindwing, though it varies dramatically across its range. It has a slow, drifting flight and is recognised by predators as toxic throughout its range.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — wild cherry, poplar, willow; Adults — sap, rotting fruit, dung
interesting_fact: The red-spotted purple is a subspecies of the white admiral that, through evolutionary pressure, has lost its white band and evolved a colour pattern mimicking the toxic pipevine swallowtail. It is a classic North American example of Batesian mimicry.
description: The red-spotted purple has broad, blue-iridescent wings above with red-orange spots on the wing underside. There is no white band (unlike its close relative the white admiral). It closely mimics the pipevine swallowtail in both wing shape and colour.

diet: Herbivore — grasses, shrubs, branches
geographic_range: Sub-Saharan Africa (South Africa, Zimbabwe, Namibia, Kenya)
interesting_fact: Rhino horn is made entirely of keratin — the same protein found in human fingernails and hair. Despite this, rhino horn is in high demand in traditional medicine markets, driving severe poaching.
description: The white rhinoceros is the largest rhinoceros species and one of the largest land animals. It is not actually white — the name derives from the Afrikaans 'wyd' (wide) for its broad, flat mouth adapted for grazing.

habitat: Paddy fields, flooded lowlands, tropical and subtropical regions
diet: N/A — plant
interesting_fact: Rice feeds more than half the world's population and has been cultivated in Asia for over 7,000 years. The flooded paddy fields used to grow rice are one of the largest human-created wetland ecosystems and support rich biodiversity including fish, frogs, and waterbirds.
threats: Climate change, water scarcity, saltwater intrusion
description: Rice is an annual grass growing 1–1.8 m tall in waterlogged fields (paddies). Its edible grain (the rice seed) is the staple food of most of Asia. There are over 40,000 varieties adapted to diverse climates and soil conditions.

habitat: Maple forests and woodlands of eastern North America
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — maple trees; Adults — cannot eat
interesting_fact: The rosy maple moth is frequently called the most beautiful moth in North America. Its absurdly vibrant pink and yellow colouring is thought to be aposematic — warning predators of unpalatability — though the exact mechanism of its apparent protection is still studied.
description: The rosy maple moth is a small to medium-sized moth, strikingly coloured with vivid flamingo-pink wings and body with bright yellow patches — a combination that seems impossible in nature. Males have distinctively feathered antennae.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — blackthorn, cherry, apple; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: The scarce swallowtail (podalirius swallowtail) is the largest butterfly in much of central Europe. Despite its name, it is actually quite common in its range. Its long tails and bold striping make it one of the most elegant and recognisable butterflies in Europe.
threats: Loss of blackthorn scrub, orchard decline, pesticides
description: The scarce swallowtail has pale cream-yellow wings beautifully streaked with black, vivid blue hindwing markings, red eyespots, and long, elegant hindwing tails. Its flight is soaring and effortless — one of the most graceful butterflies in Europe.

habitat: Rocky coasts, sandy beaches, ice floes
diet: Carnivore — fish, squid, crustaceans
interesting_fact: Grey seals can hold their breath for up to 30 minutes and dive to depths exceeding 300 m. Their whiskers (vibrissae) are so sensitive that they can detect prey movements in the water without using sight.
description: The grey seal is a large true seal with a distinctive elongated, 'Roman nose' profile (more pronounced in males). Males are slate-grey with pale spots; females are lighter with darker spots. Males can be twice the weight of females.

diet: Carnivore — fish, turtles, birds, dolphins, other sharks
geographic_range: Worldwide in tropical and subtropical coastal waters
interesting_fact: Bull sharks are one of the few shark species that can survive in both salt and fresh water. They have been found hundreds of kilometres up rivers including the Amazon and Mississippi.
threats: Overfishing, bycatch, habitat loss, shark finning
description: The bull shark is a large, heavy-bodied shark with a broad, flat snout and is grey on top with a white belly. It is one of the three shark species most frequently involved in unprovoked attacks on humans.

diet: Herbivore — grass, hay, plants
interesting_fact: Sheep have an excellent memory for faces — they can remember up to 50 other sheep faces for over two years. Studies show they can also recognise human faces and show a preference for happy expressions over neutral ones.
description: The domestic sheep is a ruminant mammal typically covered in a thick woolly coat. One of the earliest animals to be domesticated (around 10,000 years ago), it is raised worldwide for wool, meat, and milk.

diet: Herbivore (larvae) — black locust and legumes; Adults — many flowers
interesting_fact: The silver-spotted skipper is considered the most recognisable skipper in North America. Despite being a skipper (a group often considered 'dull'), it is large, fast-flying, and has dramatic markings. Males are extremely territorial, chasing anything that passes through their perch territory, including much larger insects and even birds.
description: The silver-spotted skipper is a medium-large skipper with dark brown wings, a bold yellow-orange patch on the forewing, and a distinctive large silver spot on the hindwing underside. Skippers hold their wings at their characteristic hooked angle when resting.

habitat: Chalk and limestone grasslands, coastal cliffs, meadows
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — bird's-foot trefoil; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: The six-spot burnet produces its own hydrogen cyanide — it doesn't just acquire it from food plants, it biosynthesises it. This self-produced cyanide, stored as cyanogenic glycosides, makes it highly toxic to predators. Its vivid warning colours are honest signals.
threats: Loss of chalk grassland, reduction in bird's-foot trefoil
description: The six-spot burnet moth is a day-flying moth with striking metallic blue-black forewings bearing six bright crimson spots. The hindwings are crimson bordered in black. Its slow, buzzing flight advertises its toxicity to predators.

habitat: Open fields, roadsides, woodland edges with cassia plants
diet: Herbivore (larvae) — various Cassia species; Adults — nectar
interesting_fact: The sleepy orange is named for the black cell spot on its forewing that looks like a closed, 'sleepy' eye when the wings are folded. It is one of the few sulphur butterflies that overwinters as an adult, continuing to fly on warm winter days.
description: The sleepy orange is a bright orange butterfly with yellow-orange undersides and bold black wing borders above. It has seasonal forms — summer individuals are more vivid; winter individuals have darker, more mottled underwings for camouflage.

habitat: Deciduous forests, gardens, meadows, vineyards, hedgerows
diet: Herbivore — leaves, decaying vegetation, fungi, berries
geographic_range: Central and Southeast Europe; introduced worldwide
interesting_fact: The Roman snail can seal the entrance of its shell with a parchment-like covering (epiphragm) and hibernate for months. In dry conditions it can aestivate (summer dormancy) for years, reviving when moistened. Its slime reduces friction and is being studied for biomedical adhesives.
threats: Habitat loss, collection for food, pesticide use
description: The Roman snail (also called escargot) has a large, globular creamy-white to pale brown shell with 4–5 whorls and brown banding. The body is grey with pale tentacles topped by eyes. It produces copious mucus for locomotion and sealing the shell.

habitat: Every terrestrial and aquatic habitat except Arctic
diet: Carnivore — rodents, frogs, lizards, birds, insects, fish
geographic_range: All continents except Antarctica and Ireland
interesting_fact: Snakes have no eyelids — instead, their eyes are covered by transparent scales called brilles that are shed with the skin. Snakes 'hear' vibrations through their jawbone connected to the inner ear, and taste the air using their forked tongue.
threats: Habitat loss, persecution, road mortality, illegal trade
description: Snakes are elongated, limbless reptiles with flexible, kinetic skulls allowing them to swallow prey much larger than their head diameter. About 600 of the 3,700 species are venomous. They range from 10 cm to over 9 m in length.

habitat: Alpine and subalpine zones, rocky mountains, cliffs
diet: Carnivore — blue sheep, Himalayan tahr, deer, birds
geographic_range: Central and South Asia (Himalayas, Hindu Kush, Altai)
interesting_fact: Snow leopards use their extraordinarily long, thick tails (nearly the length of their bodies) as a blanket — wrapping them around their faces to protect from extreme cold and wind.
threats: Poaching, retaliatory killing, habitat loss, prey decline
description: The snow leopard has a pale smoky grey coat with dark rosettes and spots, a stocky body, and wide, fur-covered paws that act as natural snowshoes. It is rarely seen and is sometimes called the 'ghost of the mountains'.

diet: Omnivore — seeds, insects, berries, scraps
geographic_range: Worldwide (introduced to all continents except Antarctica)
interesting_fact: House sparrows are one of the most widespread birds on Earth, having followed human settlements across the globe. Despite being so common, house sparrow populations have declined by over 60% in the UK since the 1970s due to urban changes.
threats: Habitat change (urban intensification), food reduction, air pollution
description: The house sparrow is a small, stocky bird. Males have a grey crown, white cheeks, and a distinctive black bib; females are plain brown. It is one of the most familiar birds in the world, strongly associated with human habitation.

diet: Carnivore — insects caught in webs
geographic_range: Europe, North America, parts of Asia
interesting_fact: The garden spider's orb web is an engineering marvel — stronger than steel by weight, more elastic than nylon, and capable of absorbing the kinetic energy of flying insects without breaking. Spiders consume and rebuild their webs daily to recycle the protein.
threats: Pesticide reduction of prey, habitat loss
description: The garden spider (cross spider) is a common orb-weaving spider with a large, round abdomen typically brown with a pale cross-shaped pattern of white spots. It builds a large circular web among vegetation, typically with a characteristic zigzag of white silk (stabilimentum) in the centre.

habitat: Open ocean, coastal waters, from surface to 500m depth
diet: Carnivore — fish, crustaceans, smaller squid
interesting_fact: Squids can change colour in milliseconds using chromatophores (pigment-filled sacs), iridophores (reflective plates), and leucophores (white diffusers) in their skin — all under direct neural control. They can produce complex moving patterns for camouflage, communication, and prey attraction.
description: The European squid has a streamlined, torpedo-shaped body with two triangular fins, eight arms, and two longer tentacles. Its transparent skin is dotted with chromatophores that allow rapid, dramatic colour changes from transparent to red, brown, and mottled patterns.

diet: Omnivore — nuts, seeds, fruit, fungi, insects, eggs
geographic_range: Eastern North America; introduced to UK, Ireland, parts of Europe
interesting_fact: Grey squirrels can find nuts buried under 30 cm of snow and remember the locations of thousands of caches they made months earlier. They also practise 'deceptive caching' — pretending to bury nuts while being watched.
description: The grey squirrel is a medium-sized rodent with a bushy tail and predominantly grey fur. It is an agile climber and acrobatic jumper, storing food in the ground for winter. It has become invasive in Europe, outcompeting red squirrels.

habitat: Intertidal and subtidal rocky shores, sandy seabeds
diet: Carnivore — mussels, oysters, barnacles, snails, worms
geographic_range: Northeast Atlantic coast from Svalbard to Senegal
interesting_fact: Starfish (sea stars) digest food outside their bodies — they evert (turn inside out) their stomach, insert it into a mussel shell, dissolve and absorb the prey, then retract the stomach back inside. They can open shells barely 0.1 mm wide.
threats: Ocean warming, pollution, overharvesting of prey
description: The common starfish is a five-armed sea star typically orange to pale brown in colour. Each arm tapers to a point and is covered in rows of small tube feet on the underside used for locomotion and prey capture. It can regenerate lost arms.

diet: N/A — plant
interesting_fact: Sugarcane is the world's largest crop by mass. Brazil alone produces over 750 million tonnes per year, primarily for sugar and ethanol biofuel. The plant has been grown for sugar for at least 4,000 years — originally chewed raw in ancient New Guinea and India.
threats: Land use pressure, water use, climate change
description: Sugarcane is a tall perennial grass reaching 3–6 m, with jointed, fibrous stems (culms) rich in sucrose. After harvest, it regrows from the root stubble (ratoon cropping) for several seasons before replanting is needed.

diet: Herbivore — aquatic plants, grass, grain
geographic_range: Europe, parts of Asia; introduced worldwide
interesting_fact: Mute swans are, despite their name, not completely silent. They hiss, grunt, and make wing-beat sounds. They are also among the heaviest flying birds — a large male can weigh over 15 kg. In the UK, all unmarked mute swans on open water belong to the Crown.
threats: Lead poisoning from fishing weights, power line strikes, habitat loss
description: The mute swan is a large, all-white waterbird with an orange bill marked with a black knob at the base. It glides elegantly on water with its neck in a graceful S-curve and its wings often arched. It mates for life.

diet: Unknown
interesting_fact: This class corresponds to training data labelled 'Testa' in the dataset. The exact species identity requires verification.
description: This is a dataset class labelled 'Testa'. Species data is unavailable as this label may correspond to a test category or a colloquial/regional species name not yet verified.

diet: Unknown
interesting_fact: This class corresponds to training data labelled 'Testb' in the dataset. The exact species identity requires verification.
description: This is a dataset class labelled 'Testb'. Species data is unavailable as this label may correspond to a test category or a colloquial/regional species name not yet verified.

diet: Carnivore — deer, wild boar, gaur, water buffalo
geographic_range: South and Southeast Asia, Russian Far East
interesting_fact: No two tigers have the same stripe pattern — stripes are as unique as human fingerprints. Tigers are also the only striped big cat, and the stripes appear on their skin too, not just their fur.
threats: Poaching, habitat loss, prey depletion, human conflict
description: The tiger is the largest wild cat species and one of the most iconic animals on Earth. It has an orange coat with dark vertical stripes and a white underbelly. Solitary and primarily nocturnal, it is a powerful apex predator.

habitat: Dry forests, scrublands, hillsides, rocky terrain
diet: Herbivore — plants, flowers, fruit, occasionally carrion
interesting_fact: Tortoises are one of the longest-lived animals — some individuals exceed 150 years. The world's oldest known tortoise, Jonathan, was born around 1832 and is still alive (as of 2025) on the island of St Helena at approximately 193 years of age.
threats: Habitat loss, collection for pet trade, predation of eggs
description: Hermann's tortoise is a medium-sized tortoise with a high-domed yellowish-brown shell marked with black patches. It hibernates during winter in cooler climates. Its bony shell is an integral part of its skeleton, not a shell it can leave.

diet: Omnivore — acorns, seeds, insects, berries, small reptiles
interesting_fact: Wild turkeys can fly at speeds of up to 88 km/h and have excellent vision — their field of view is 270 degrees. Despite their reputation, they are intelligent birds that have been observed using simple problem-solving strategies.
description: The wild turkey has dark, iridescent feathers with a metallic sheen, a bare, wrinkled red head, and a distinctive fan-shaped tail. Males (toms) display with spread tails and a distinctive 'gobble' call to attract females.

habitat: Arctic and sub-arctic seas, ice floes, rocky shores
diet: Carnivore — clams, sea cucumbers, worms, slow fish
geographic_range: Arctic Ocean and subarctic seas of the Northern Hemisphere
interesting_fact: Walruses use their iconic tusks (elongated canine teeth) as hooks to haul their massive bodies out of the water onto ice, to establish dominance, and as weapons in fights. Both males and females grow tusks.
threats: Climate change (sea ice loss), hunting, disturbance
description: The walrus is a large, flippered marine mammal with a distinctive wrinkled, brown-grey skin, prominent whiskers (vibrissae) used to detect molluscs, and long ivory tusks. It is highly gregarious, forming herds of thousands.

habitat: Open oceans, deep sea, polar seas
diet: Filter feeder — krill (primarily)
interesting_fact: The blue whale is the largest animal ever known to have existed on Earth. Its heart is the size of a small car, and its call — at 188 decibels — is louder than a jet engine and can be heard by other blue whales 1,600 km away.
threats: Historical whaling, ship strikes, entanglement in fishing gear, noise pollution
description: The blue whale is the largest animal on Earth. It has a long, streamlined body of blue-grey colour with a mottled pattern. Despite its size, it feeds exclusively on tiny krill, consuming up to 4 tonnes per day.

diet: N/A — plant
interesting_fact: Wheat was one of the first crops domesticated by humans, approximately 10,000 years ago in the Fertile Crescent. It is now the world's second most produced cereal after maize and the most widely consumed crop globally by humans.
threats: Climate change, disease (wheat rusts), genetic homogeneity
description: Common wheat is an annual grass growing 60–120 cm tall with erect spikes of grain-bearing florets. Its seeds (grains) are ground into flour for bread, pasta, and baked goods. Wheat accounts for approximately 20% of all calories consumed by humans worldwide.

diet: Carnivore — deer, elk, bison, moose, smaller mammals
interesting_fact: Wolf howls can carry over 16 km in open terrain. Each wolf has a distinct howl — like a fingerprint — and wolves use howling to communicate territory, locate pack members, and coordinate hunts.
threats: Habitat loss, hunting, persecution by livestock farmers
description: The grey wolf is the largest wild member of the dog family. It has a thick, bushy coat ranging from grey to brown, black, or white. Highly social, it lives and hunts in family-based packs led by an alpha pair.

diet: Herbivore — grasses, leaves, bark
interesting_fact: No two zebras have the same stripe pattern, just like human fingerprints. Scientists believe stripes may serve multiple purposes: camouflage in long grass, heat dissipation, and discouraging biting flies.
threats: Habitat loss, competition with livestock, hunting
description: The plains zebra is a member of the horse family with a distinctive black-and-white striped coat. It lives in large herds and is one of the most recognisable animals in Africa, often mixing with wildebeest and antelope on migrations.
